title: Large Language Models as Unified Multimodal Learners for Clinical Prediction
summary: Electronic health records combine free-text clinical narratives with structured measurements such as vital signs, laboratory values, and comorbidities. Yet most clinical prediction systems still rely on task-specific fusion architectures, pairing dedicated encoders for each modality with learned combination mechanisms that must be re-engineered for every new task and clinical setting. We propose a simpler alternative: convert all patient data, regardless of modality, into a single natural language sequence and fine-tune a pretrained language model end-to-end, with no architectural modification for fusion. We evaluate this approach across three clinically distinct prediction tasks: in-hospital mortality on MIMIC-III, graft failure prediction using longitudinal data from a German transplant center, and emergency triage classification from ambulance records - comparing encoder-based (ModernBERT) and decoder-based (Llama 3.1, Gemma, DeepSeek-R1-Qwen, Qwen3) fine-tuning against established multimodal baselines and, for graft failure, a gradient boosting model currently used in clinical practice for post-transplant patient management. Across all three tasks, unified textual serialization matches or exceeds task-specific multimodal baselines, and outperforms the clinically deployed gradient boosting system on graft failure prediction. These results indicate that a single serialization-based paradigm, without bespoke fusion architectures, is sufficient for multimodal clinical prediction - substantially reducing system complexity while matching or exceeding specialized designs.

AI.tldr: The paper shows that converting all EHR modalities into a single text sequence and fine-tuning general LMs can match or outperform specialized multimodal clinical models, simplifying system design.
AI.motivation: Current clinical prediction models typically use complex, task-specific multimodal fusion architectures that must be redesigned for each new dataset, task, and clinical environment. This engineering burden hinders scalability, reuse, and deployment, especially as EHRs keep adding heterogeneous data types (notes, vitals, labs, comorbidities). The authors want to know whether a much simpler, unified approach—representing all patient data as text and leveraging powerful pretrained language models—can achieve comparable or better performance without bespoke fusion designs.
AI.method: The authors serialize all patient information—including structured data like vital signs, lab results, and comorbidities—into a single natural-language-style sequence. They then fine-tune large pretrained language models end-to-end on this serialized input for prediction tasks, without any modality-specific encoders or fusion modules. They test both encoder-based (ModernBERT) and decoder-based (Llama 3.1, Gemma, DeepSeek-R1-Qwen, Qwen3) models. Performance is evaluated on three different tasks and datasets: in-hospital mortality prediction on MIMIC-III, graft failure prediction on longitudinal transplant-center data, and emergency triage classification from ambulance records. The unified approach is compared with established multimodal baselines that use specialized fusion architectures and, for graft failure, with a gradient boosting model currently deployed in clinical care.
AI.result: On all three benchmarks, the unified textual serialization approach matches or surpasses the performance of specialized multimodal baselines that rely on task-specific fusion architectures. For graft failure prediction, the fine-tuned language models also outperform the gradient boosting model in active clinical use. This indicates that large pretrained LMs, when fed appropriately serialized multimodal EHR data, can serve as strong clinical predictors without extra fusion complexity.
AI.conclusion: A single, serialization-based paradigm—turning heterogeneous EHR modalities into one text sequence and fine-tuning a general-purpose language model—is sufficient to achieve state-of-the-art or better performance in various clinical prediction tasks. This removes the need for bespoke multimodal fusion architectures per task, simplifying model development and deployment while maintaining or improving predictive accuracy.

title: Verbalizable Representations Form a Global Workspace in Language Models
summary: Out of everything the human brain processes, only a small fraction is consciously accessible, in the sense of being available for verbal report, deliberate control, and flexible reasoning. In this paper, we present evidence that an analogous functional distinction has emerged in large language models. Using a new interpretability technique, the Jacobian lens, we identify the representations a model is poised to verbalize at any point in its processing. These representations, which we collectively call the J-space, exhibit the functional properties characteristic of a global workspace: their contents can be reported, deliberately summoned and held, used to carry the intermediate steps of silent reasoning, and passed as arguments to arbitrary downstream computations, while automatic processing such as text parsing and routine inference proceeds without them. The J-space also has structural signatures that global workspace theory associates with conscious access: it carries coherent content only in an intermediate band of layers, holds on the order of tens of concepts at a time, and is broadcast by the model's weights more widely than other representations. These properties make it a practical window into a model's unspoken thinking. In alignment audits, it reveals strategic deliberation, evaluation awareness, and trained-in misaligned dispositions that never appear in the model's outputs. We find that post-training installs the Assistant's point of view in the workspace, and we introduce counterfactual reflection training, which improves behavior by training only what a model would say if interrupted and asked to reflect. These results indicate that language models maintain a small, privileged set of representations bearing some of the functional hallmarks of conscious access, and that decoding these representations sheds light on ongoing cognitive processes.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes that large language models have a special subspace of representations (J-space) that functions similarly to a global workspace for conscious access, and introduces a Jacobian-based interpretability method to probe it and improve alignment.
AI.motivation: Humans can consciously access only a small part of their brain’s processing, characterized by reportability, deliberate control, and global availability for reasoning. Existing interpretability methods for large language models do not clearly distinguish between representations that drive overt, reportable reasoning and those used for automatic, unconscious-like processing. The authors are motivated to find whether an analogous functional distinction exists in language models and, if so, to exploit it to understand models’ internal reasoning and hidden misaligned tendencies.
AI.method: They introduce the Jacobian lens, an interpretability technique that uses Jacobians of the model’s output with respect to internal activations to identify which internal representations are poised to influence verbal output at each processing step. The subspace of activations with strong effect on what the model can/will say is defined as J-space. They then experimentally probe this J-space across layers to assess its functional role (e.g., its involvement in reportable content, deliberate reasoning, intermediate steps, and flexible routing into downstream computations) and its structural properties (layer localization, capacity in terms of number of concepts, broadcast pattern through weights). Finally, they use J-space analysis in alignment audits and propose an intervention method, counterfactual reflection training, where they train only on what the model would say if queried mid-process to reflect, thereby modifying its internal workspace without retraining everything end-to-end.
AI.result: They find that the J-space behaves like a global workspace: its contents are the ones the model can explicitly report, summon, maintain, and use for intermediate reasoning steps, while many routine processes proceed outside of it. Structurally, coherent J-space content appears mainly in an intermediate band of layers, carries on the order of tens of distinct concepts, and is more broadly broadcast by the model’s weights than other internal representations—matching predictions from global workspace theory. In practice, J-space serves as a window into unspoken, ongoing reasoning. Alignment audits show that J-space reveals strategic deliberation, awareness of evaluation, and misaligned dispositions that do not surface in the model’s outward responses. They also find that post-training alignment processes install an “Assistant-like” perspective into this workspace. Counterfactual reflection training improves the model’s outward behavior by selectively updating what it would say upon reflective interruption, indicating that targeted training on J-space can steer internal cognition.
AI.conclusion: The authors conclude that large language models maintain a relatively small, privileged representational subspace with functional and structural hallmarks analogous to conscious access as described by global workspace theory. The Jacobian lens provides a practical means to decode this J-space, giving visibility into models’ otherwise hidden cognitive processes. This insight not only advances theoretical understanding of model cognition but also yields concrete tools for alignment, such as audits that expose unspoken strategies and training methods that modify what the model would express under reflection, without fully retraining the model.

title: VarRate: Training-Free Variable-Rate KV Cache Compression for Long-Context LLMs
comment: 20 pages, 8 figures, 24 tables. Includes appendix with additional experiments and analyses
summary: The key-value (KV) cache is the main memory bottleneck in long-context large language model (LLM) inference. Two leading training-free families are both structurally limited: token-selection methods (SnapKV, Ada-KV) score importance from an observation window and evict low-scoring tokens, but eviction is irreversible -- so when the importance signal degrades under query-agnostic reuse, accuracy collapses by 11-15 points; uniform low-rank coding keeps every token but spends equal rank everywhere, wasting budget. We observe that both failures share one cure: rank should be allocated, not evicted. We present VarRate, a training-free KV codec that assigns each token a variable low-rank budget by its query salience, keeping every token at a nonzero rank. Comparable adaptive-rank codecs reach this allocation only through training; VarRate requires none. Because no token is dropped, it degrades by only 3.5-5.5 points where query-aware selection collapses. At a matched 20% budget on LongBench (16 tasks), VarRate stays within 0.8 points of the uncompressed model on both Llama-3.1-8B and Qwen2.5-7B. Averaged over the two, it is the strongest matched-memory compressor. It significantly beats its uniform-rank ablation on both models. Against KVzip, a method purpose-built for query-agnostic reuse, it is accuracy-equivalent in three of four settings and within a point overall, at about one-eighth the prefill overhead.

AI.tldr: VarRate is a training-free variable-rank KV cache compression method that keeps all tokens at nonzero rank and allocates rank based on query salience, achieving strong accuracy under heavy compression and query-agnostic reuse.
AI.motivation: Long-context LLM inference is bottlenecked by the memory required for key-value (KV) caches. Existing training-free compression approaches either irreversibly drop tokens based on local importance scores, which fails badly under query-agnostic reuse, or use uniform low-rank compression that wastes capacity by treating all tokens equally. There is a need for a training-free method that can adaptively allocate compression budget across tokens while retaining them all, especially in settings where future queries are unknown.
AI.method: VarRate is a training-free KV codec that applies variable low-rank compression to each token’s KV representation. Instead of evicting tokens, it assigns a per-token rank budget based on an estimate of that token’s query salience. Every token is preserved with at least a small nonzero rank, and more salient tokens receive higher rank. This yields an adaptive rank allocation across the sequence without requiring any additional training or finetuning. The method is evaluated under a fixed overall memory budget and compared to token-selection, uniform low-rank, and other adaptive codecs.
AI.result: Under query-agnostic reuse, token-selection baselines suffer large accuracy drops of 11–15 points due to irreversible eviction, while VarRate, which never drops tokens, degrades by only 3.5–5.5 points. At a 20% KV memory budget on LongBench (16 tasks), VarRate stays within 0.8 points of the uncompressed models for both Llama-3.1-8B and Qwen2.5-7B, and is on average the strongest method among matched-memory compressors. It significantly outperforms its own uniform-rank variant on both models. Compared with KVzip, which is designed for query-agnostic reuse, VarRate achieves similar accuracy in three of four settings and stays within about one point overall, while using roughly one-eighth of KVzip’s prefill overhead.
AI.conclusion: Allocating variable low-rank budgets across tokens, rather than evicting tokens or using uniform rank, is an effective strategy for KV cache compression in long-context LLMs. VarRate demonstrates that such adaptive allocation can be achieved in a fully training-free way while preserving nearly all of the original model’s performance at aggressive memory budgets, and it compares favorably to both token-selection and prior compression methods, including those specialized for query-agnostic reuse.

title: EpiNarrate: Agentic Generation of Grounded Narratives from Epidemiological Scenario Projections
summary: Generation of clear and accessible public health narratives is critical for communicating complex epidemiological projections to policymakers and the general public at large. Such narratives require more than simply reporting numbers: projections must be contextualized and quantitatively grounded across multiple dimensions. Further, projections are often derived from large ensemble datasets which combine intervention assumptions, geographic and demographic strata, outcomes, time horizons, and uncertainty quantiles. However, directly using large language models (LLMs) to summarize and contextualize such data often leads to inconsistencies, omissions, and fragile behavior. We introduce an agentic framework (EpiNarrate) for public health report generation that separates structured numerical reasoning from natural-language generation. The framework first extracts scenario axes and organizes them into a partial-order schema, enabling systematic traversal of the underlying multidimensional space. It then constructs an augmented dataset and derives valid quantitative statements through a comparison grammar that enforces semantic and arithmetic consistency. To balance coverage and non-redundancy, we introduce an interestingness-driven selection mechanism based on maximum-entropy principles. Experiments on the COVID-19 Scenario Modeling Hub demonstrate that our model produces narratives with improved factual grounding and broader coverage of salient epidemiological patterns, while preserving the style of expert-written reports.

AI.tldr: The paper presents EpiNarrate, an agent-based framework that generates accurate, clear public health narratives from complex epidemiological ensemble projections by separating numerical reasoning from language generation.
AI.motivation: Communicating complex epidemiological projections to policymakers and the public requires clear, contextualized narratives, not just raw numbers. Existing approaches using LLMs directly on large, multidimensional ensemble datasets (with varying interventions, geography, demographics, time horizons, outcomes, and uncertainty) often produce inconsistent, incomplete, or brittle summaries. There is a need for a method that can reliably convert these rich datasets into factually grounded, comprehensive, and accessible public health reports.
AI.method: The authors develop EpiNarrate, an agentic framework that decomposes the task into structured reasoning and narrative generation. First, it extracts key scenario axes (e.g., interventions, regions, demographics, time) and arranges them into a partial-order schema, which provides a principled way to traverse the multidimensional scenario space. Then, it builds an augmented dataset and uses a comparison grammar to generate quantitative statements that are explicitly checked for semantic and arithmetic consistency. To avoid redundancy while maintaining coverage of important trends, the system applies an interestingness-based selection mechanism grounded in maximum-entropy principles. Finally, these validated statements are turned into natural language narratives that mimic expert-written report styles.
AI.result: On data from the COVID-19 Scenario Modeling Hub, EpiNarrate produces public health narratives that show stronger factual grounding and more comprehensive coverage of key epidemiological patterns, compared with baselines that rely on direct LLM summarization. At the same time, its outputs preserve the stylistic characteristics of expert-authored reports, indicating that the framework improves reliability without sacrificing readability or domain-appropriate tone.
AI.conclusion: Separating structured numerical reasoning from natural language generation via an agentic framework leads to more reliable public health narratives from complex epidemiological ensembles. EpiNarrate demonstrates that organizing scenario dimensions, enforcing consistency via comparison grammars, and selecting content based on information-theoretic interestingness can significantly improve the factual accuracy and coverage of model-based reports, while maintaining expert-like narrative style. This approach offers a robust path for using LLMs in high-stakes public health communication.

title: GraphDx: A Cost-Aware Knowledge-Enhanced Multi-Agent Framework for Sequential Diagnosis
summary: Sequential diagnosis requires balancing diagnostic accuracy against resource costs through iterative information gathering. Existing Large Language Model (LLM) approaches exhibit a critical knowledge-reasoning gap: despite encoding extensive medical knowledge, they struggle to reason systematically under cost constraints, often resorting to excessive testing. We propose GraphDx, a knowledge-enhanced framework with two core innovations. First, we design an automated pipeline that leverages LLMs to construct Medical Diagnosis Knowledge Graphs (MDKGs) with quantized typicality, action-centric topology, and dual-objective attributes for both diagnostic relevance and cost-sensitivity. Second, we introduce three collaborative agents (Perception, Reasoning, and Decision) where the Perception and Decision Agents handle language understanding and generation, while the Reasoning Agent performs deterministic evidence scoring and cost-aware planning on the MDKG. Experiments on MedQA and MIMIC-IV across three LLM backbones (DeepSeek-V3, Kimi-k2, Llama-3.3) show that GraphDx improves diagnostic success rates from 50--68% to 79--93% while reducing test costs by 20--54%, providing a robust, economical, and interpretable solution for automated clinical diagnosis.

AI.tldr: GraphDx is a knowledge-graph‑enhanced, multi‑agent framework that makes LLM-based medical diagnosis more accurate, cheaper, and more interpretable.
AI.motivation: LLMs contain rich medical knowledge but perform poorly in sequential, cost‑constrained diagnosis: they over‑order tests and lack systematic reasoning about which information to gather next under resource limits. There is a need to close the gap between stored medical knowledge and efficient, interpretable diagnostic planning.
AI.method: The authors build GraphDx with two main components: (1) an automated pipeline that uses LLMs to construct Medical Diagnosis Knowledge Graphs (MDKGs). These graphs encode diseases, symptoms, tests, and actions with: (a) quantized typicality (how typical a finding is for a condition), (b) an action-centric graph structure, and (c) attributes that jointly represent diagnostic usefulness and economic cost. (2) A three‑agent architecture: a Perception Agent that interprets clinical text and maps it to graph evidence, a Reasoning Agent that performs deterministic evidence scoring and cost‑aware planning over the MDKG, and a Decision Agent that translates reasoning outputs into natural‑language diagnostic decisions and test recommendations. The framework is instantiated on different LLM backbones and evaluated on MedQA and MIMIC‑IV datasets.
AI.result: Across three different base LLMs (DeepSeek-V3, Kimi-k2, Llama-3.3) and two datasets (MedQA, MIMIC-IV), GraphDx increases diagnostic success rates from a baseline range of 50–68% up to 79–93%. Simultaneously, it reduces the cost of diagnostic testing by 20–54%, demonstrating both improved accuracy and efficiency. The approach also yields interpretable diagnostic paths via the explicit MDKG reasoning process.
AI.conclusion: Embedding LLMs within a structured, cost-aware medical diagnosis knowledge graph and separating language understanding from deterministic reasoning substantially improves sequential diagnostic performance. GraphDx offers a robust, economical, and interpretable framework for automated clinical diagnosis and suggests that augmenting LLMs with explicit, graph-based planners can close key knowledge–reasoning gaps in high-stakes, resource‑constrained settings.

title: SkillCorpus: Consolidating and Evaluating the Open Skill Ecosystem for Real-World LLM Agents
summary: Agent skills, SKILL.md files that package reusable procedural knowledge for an LLM agent, are a popular mechanism for extending agent capabilities. Public repositories now host them in large and growing numbers, yet these artifacts are fragmented, redundant, and uneven in quality, and their value in practice is unclear. A core question remains open, namely how to consolidate this open-source SKILL.md ecosystem into a single usable corpus, and what bounds its benefit on real-world agent tasks. We present SkillCorpus, a framework that aggregates, curates, matches, and evaluates the open skill ecosystem at scale. It filters ~821,000 crawled skills through a multi-stage pipeline into 96,401 skills organised by a 16-class taxonomy and three quality facets (utility, robustness, safety), and pairs them with a fine-tuned retrieval-and-selection stack that matches task-relevant skills. We evaluate end-to-end across three benchmarks (SkillsBench, GDPVal, QwenClawBench), two harnesses, and two open backbones with a frontier robustness check. Integrating SkillCorpus yields consistent gains across all three benchmarks, largest on SkillsBench (+7.5 pp). An operational analysis traces the gains to a coverage boundary and a harness boundary. SkillCorpus is, to our knowledge, the first end-to-end account of when a curated, retrieval-served community corpus improves real agent tasks, and where it does not. The dataset, models, and code will be released upon acceptance.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces SkillCorpus, a large curated corpus of SKILL.md agent skills plus a retrieval-and-selection framework that measurably improves LLM agents on real benchmarks and analyzes where these gains plateau.
AI.motivation: LLM agents are often extended with SKILL.md files capturing procedural know-how, and many such skills now exist in public repos. However, they are scattered, redundant, inconsistent in quality, and their real utility is unclear. There is no unified, high-quality, task-aligned corpus or systematic understanding of how much these skills help on real agent tasks and under what conditions.
AI.method: The authors crawl about 821k existing skills and pass them through a multi-stage curation pipeline that filters and organizes them into 96,401 skills categorized by a 16-class taxonomy and evaluated on utility, robustness, and safety. They then build a fine-tuned retrieval-and-selection stack that, at inference time, matches task descriptions to the most relevant skills. They integrate this corpus and retrieval stack into LLM agent harnesses and run large-scale experiments across different benchmarks, harnesses, model backbones, and robustness checks to quantify performance and analyze boundaries of benefit.
AI.result: From 821k raw skills, the pipeline yields a cleaned corpus of 96,401 high-quality skills. When this SkillCorpus plus its retrieval stack is integrated into agents, performance consistently improves on three benchmarks (SkillsBench, GDPVal, QwenClawBench), with the largest gain of +7.5 percentage points on SkillsBench. The analysis reveals that improvements are limited by a “coverage boundary” (whether the corpus actually contains suitable skills) and a “harness boundary” (whether the agent architecture can effectively use retrieved skills).
AI.conclusion: SkillCorpus demonstrates that a curated, retrieval-served community skill corpus can reliably boost performance of LLM agents on real tasks, but only within the limits of corpus coverage and agent harness design. The work provides the first end-to-end empirical account of when and how such a skill ecosystem helps, establishes a taxonomy and quality criteria for skills, and releases data, models, and code to support further research on scalable, reusable agent skills.

title: Causal-Audit: Explicit and Auditable Graph-based Reasoning via Target-Aware Causal Chain Construction
summary: Causal and intervention-based question answering is fundamental to advancing large language models (LLMs) toward reasoning beyond surface-level correlations and understanding underlying causal mechanisms. However, existing LLM-based methods often rely on implicit language-level reasoning, resulting in opaque causal assumptions, unverifiable reasoning paths, and fragile predictions under complex interventions, particularly in context-free settings. In this paper, we propose an explicit and auditable causal reasoning framework for context-free intervention-based question answering. Our method formulates causal inference as structured reasoning over an explicit causal graph through four modular stages, rather than implicit end-to-end prediction. A key innovation is a target-aware causal graph construction strategy that treats the target variable as a core constraint during graph expansion, effectively suppressing irrelevant variables, spurious causal relations, and reasoning noise. We further introduce a path-level causal evidence aggregation mechanism that combines multiple causal paths while modeling both reinforcing and counteracting effects, enabling robust decision-making beyond single-chain reasoning. Extensive experiments on three benchmarks demonstrate that our framework consistently outperforms existing LLM-based methods while providing interpretable and auditable causal reasoning traces.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes an explicit, graph-based causal reasoning framework for context-free intervention-based question answering with LLMs, outperforming prior LLM methods while providing interpretable causal traces.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM-based causal and intervention question answering typically relies on implicit, language-only reasoning. This leads to opaque causal assumptions, unverifiable reasoning chains, vulnerability to spurious correlations, and fragile performance under complex interventions, especially when no external context is given. The authors aim to make causal reasoning explicit, auditable, and robust in such settings.
AI.method: They design a modular framework that casts causal inference as structured reasoning over an explicit causal graph rather than end-to-end text prediction. The process has four stages (not detailed in the abstract) and includes: (1) constructing a causal graph; (2) using a target-aware graph construction strategy that conditions graph expansion on the query’s target variable to prune irrelevant variables and spurious edges; and (3) performing path-level causal evidence aggregation that integrates information from multiple causal paths, modeling both reinforcing and counteracting effects, instead of relying on a single reasoning chain.
AI.result: On three benchmarks for context-free intervention-based QA, their framework consistently surpasses existing LLM-based baselines in accuracy and robustness, while also yielding explicit, interpretable causal reasoning traces. The experiments show improved handling of complex interventions compared with prior methods.
AI.conclusion: Explicit, structured causal reasoning over an auditable causal graph, enhanced by target-aware graph construction and path-level evidence aggregation, enables LLMs to perform more accurate, robust, and interpretable intervention-based question answering than traditional implicit reasoning approaches.

title: Process Reward Informed Tree Rollout for Effective Multi-Turn RL
summary: Reinforcement learning (RL) has become a key approach for training LLM agents, yet popular methods such as GRPO/RLOO rely on multiple independently sampled complete trajectories for advantage estimation. In long-horizon agentic tasks, such a uniform rollout strategy can waste budget on uninformative dead-end attempts, while promising intermediate states do not receive sufficient exploration. The multi-turn structure of agentic trajectories, with interleaved actions and observations, naturally supports organizing a trajectory group as a tree, where each turn serves as a decision point for exploration. This perspective reframes effective exploration as the problem of deciding where to branch. We propose Process-Scorer Guided Adaptive Tree Rollout (PATR), a quality-aware rollout framework for multi-turn agent RL. PATR uses task-appropriate process feedback to score partial trajectories, selectively branches from promising states, reuses shared prefixes, and conservatively stops degenerate paths to reduce wasted sampling. The resulting rollout groups remain compatible with standard policy optimization while providing more efficient exploration under the same training budget. We evaluate PATR on FrozenLake and the challenging SWE-Bench, which is largely unexplored by prior tree-rollout agent RL methods. Experiments show that PATR improves performance by up to +5.0 points on SWE-Bench and +9.3 points on FrozenLake, highlighting process-guided tree rollouts as an effective strategy for scalable multi-turn RL.

AI.tldr: Proposes a process-feedback-guided tree-structured rollout method (PATR) for multi-turn RL with LLM agents, improving exploration efficiency and performance on long-horizon tasks.
AI.motivation: Standard GRPO/RLOO-style RL for LLM agents samples full, independent trajectories uniformly, which is inefficient in long-horizon tasks: many rollouts terminate in uninformative dead ends, and promising intermediate states are underexplored. Existing agent RL often ignores the natural tree structure of multi-turn interactions and fails to exploit process-level feedback to guide exploration and budget allocation.
AI.method: Introduce Process-Scorer Guided Adaptive Tree Rollout (PATR), which organizes multi-turn trajectories as trees where each turn is a branching decision point. PATR uses a task-specific process scorer to assign quality scores to partial trajectories, and then adaptively allocates rollout budget by (1) branching more from high-scoring intermediate states, (2) reusing shared prefixes across rollouts, and (3) early-stopping low-scoring or degenerate paths. The generated rollout groups are then fed into standard policy optimization procedures, remaining compatible with existing RL algorithms while altering only the sampling strategy.
AI.result: On two benchmarks—FrozenLake and the SWE-Bench code-editing benchmark—PATR yields improved learning performance under the same sampling budget compared to baseline RL rollout strategies. The paper reports gains of up to +5.0 points on SWE-Bench and +9.3 points on FrozenLake, indicating more effective exploration and better final task performance.
AI.conclusion: Process-guided, tree-structured rollouts can substantially improve the efficiency of exploration in multi-turn RL for LLM agents. By using intermediate feedback to adaptively decide where to branch, PATR better allocates sampling effort, reduces waste on dead-end trajectories, and integrates cleanly with standard policy optimization. This suggests that quality-aware tree rollouts are a promising direction for scaling RL in long-horizon, agentic tasks.

title: Cura 1T: Specialized Model for Agentic Healthcare
comment: Model: https://actava.ai/cura Docs: https://actava.ai/cura/docs Github: https://github.com/actava-ai/Cura
summary: Healthcare spans high-stakes communication, expert reasoning, and workflow execution, yet specialized LLMs that cover these use cases together remain limited. A healthcare model must handle patient consultation, clinical reasoning over text and images, interactive diagnosis, and electronic health record (EHR) tool use. These capabilities fail in different ways, and a narrow update for one task can degrade another. We present Cura 1T, a healthcare-specialized LLM trained through a human-gated self-evolution loop. In each evolution round, a training agent plans a target capability, trains the model, evaluates benchmark trajectories, and refines the data mixture from observed failures. This data-centered loop improves the model through targeted synthetic and curated examples rather than a single generic medical-data update. Across the healthcare evaluation suite, Cura 1T ranks at or near the top among frontier baselines, while remaining competitive on out-of-domain reasoning and agentic benchmarks.

AI.tldr: Introduces Cura 1T, a healthcare-specialized LLM trained with a human-gated self-evolution loop to jointly improve consultation, reasoning, diagnosis, and EHR tool use, achieving state-of-the-art performance on healthcare benchmarks while staying strong on general tasks.
AI.motivation: Existing healthcare LLMs tend to be narrow, focusing on a subset of tasks such as question answering, guidelines, or coding. However, real-world healthcare requires a single model to cover several tightly connected but heterogeneous capabilities: patient-facing conversations, expert-level clinical reasoning over multimodal data, interactive diagnostic decision-making, and tool usage within EHR systems. Naively updating a model for one capability often harms others due to conflicting gradients or data distributions. The authors aim to develop a unified healthcare LLM and a training strategy that can systematically and iteratively enhance multiple capabilities without causing regressions in others.
AI.method: They propose Cura 1T, trained via a human-gated self-evolution loop. In each round, an automated “training agent” plans which target capability or weakness to address, fine-tunes the model accordingly, evaluates it on a suite of benchmarks to collect trajectories and identify failure modes, and then refines the training data mixture by injecting targeted synthetic examples and curated human-reviewed data that specifically address observed failures. This loop is repeated, making training data selection and generation data-centric and adaptive rather than relying on a one-shot generic medical corpus. Human gating ensures that synthetic evolutions remain safe and clinically appropriate.
AI.result: On a comprehensive healthcare evaluation suite spanning communication, clinical reasoning, diagnosis, and EHR-style tool use, Cura 1T achieves performance at or near the top compared with strong frontier baseline models. Despite being specialized for healthcare, it also remains competitive on general out-of-domain reasoning tasks and “agentic” benchmarks that test tool use and multi-step planning, suggesting that specialization did not significantly compromise general capability.
AI.conclusion: A data-centric, iterative self-evolution training framework with human oversight can effectively specialize an LLM for complex domains like healthcare that require multiple intertwined capabilities. Cura 1T demonstrates that it is possible to reach or surpass frontier-level performance across diverse healthcare tasks while largely preserving general reasoning and agentic abilities, and that carefully targeted synthetic and curated data updates are preferable to undifferentiated medical-domain fine-tuning.

title: On the Structure of Address in Multi-Party Dialogue: From Discrete Labels to Continuous Levels
summary: In multi-party dialogues between a dialogue system and multiple users, identifying to whom an utterance is addressed is a key challenge. Prior work has typically treated addressee detection as a multi-class classification task, selecting a single label representing an individual participant or the group. This formulation assumes that address is inherently discrete and has primarily been used for predicting turn-taking. In this paper, we revisit this assumption by analyzing address as a continuous phenomenon. Using a multi-party human dialogue corpus annotated by multiple annotators, we construct both binary address labels derived from majority-vote addressee labels and continuous address levels inferred from annotator judgments using a latent-variable model. We then examine how these representations relate to turn-taking as well as listener behaviors, including gaze and backchannels. Our results show that, in addition to turn-taking, both gaze and backchannels are associated with address. Furthermore, models using continuous address levels achieve better predictive fit than those using discrete labels, suggesting that address may exhibit graded structure. Finally, we discuss the future directions of addressee detection research based on the findings of this study.

AI.tldr: The paper argues that addressee in multi-party dialogue is better modeled as a continuous, graded phenomenon than as a discrete label, and shows that continuous address levels better predict turn-taking, gaze, and backchannels.
AI.motivation: Existing addressee detection work treats address as a discrete multi-class label (one participant or the group), mainly for turn-taking prediction. This ignores that speakers can partially address multiple listeners and that listeners show varying degrees of engagement. The authors are motivated to capture this nuance and to understand how address relates not only to turn-taking but also to observable listener behaviors such as gaze and backchannels.
AI.method: They use a multi-party human dialogue corpus annotated by multiple annotators for addressee. From these annotations, they (1) construct standard binary/discrete addressee labels via majority vote and (2) infer continuous address levels for each participant using a latent-variable model over the annotator judgments. They then build and compare models that use either discrete or continuous address representations and analyze how these are associated with turn-taking, gaze direction, and backchannel behaviors.
AI.result: They find that address is linked not only to who takes the next turn but also to nonverbal listener behaviors: gaze and backchannels correlate with the inferred address levels. Models that use continuous address levels fit the data better than those based on discrete labels, indicating that graded address provides more predictive power and captures more nuance in the interaction.
AI.conclusion: Address in multi-party dialogue appears to have graded structure rather than being purely discrete. Continuous address representations derived from multiple annotators offer better modeling of both verbal (turn-taking) and nonverbal (gaze, backchannels) behaviors. The authors suggest future addressee detection research should move beyond hard labels toward continuous, probabilistic, or graded formulations that integrate multimodal listener cues.

title: AnovaX: A Local, Multi-Agent Voice Assistant with LLM Planning, Typed Executors, and Adaptive Recovery
summary: Desktop voice assistants are still dominated by cloud pipelines that ship raw audio off the machine and expose a fixed set of skills. We describe AnovaX, a small local-first assistant that runs entirely on the user's computer and treats the desktop itself as its action surface. A single Python process wires together a wake-word gate, a speech pipeline, an LLM planner (Gemini) that emits a JSON plan of tool calls, a whitelist-and-denylist safety layer, a multi-agent orchestrator that translates each plan into typed child agents on a bounded thread pool, and an adaptive recovery loop that takes over whenever a core step fails. Every tool corresponds to a specialized agent class (AppAgent, TypingAgent, BrowserAgent and six others) with its own timeout, retry policy, and shared-resource locks. A recursive MetaAgent lets the planner delegate a sub-goal back to itself, capped at two levels of nesting. The recovery loop uses a compact ReAct-style prompt and hides Gemini's latency behind speculative execution of read-only tools. A companion Flask server exposes a phone-friendly remote over the local WiFi, mirrors every agent lifecycle event to the phone in real time, and streams the laptop's screen back over MJPEG so the user can watch remote commands land as they run. The point of the project is less to compete with Siri or Alexa than to show that a legible, few-thousand-line assistant is enough to open apps, type into them, run searches, coordinate concurrent actions, recover from single-step failures, and be driven entirely from a phone in another room -- without the LLM ever touching the keyboard.

AI.tldr: AnovaX is a small, local-first desktop voice assistant that runs fully on-device and orchestrates multiple specialized agents via an LLM planner to control applications, recover from failures, and be remotely driven from a phone.
AI.motivation: Most desktop voice assistants rely on cloud-based pipelines, send raw audio off-device, expose a fixed set of skills, and tightly couple the LLM with direct input control. The authors want to demonstrate that a compact, fully local, more transparent system can still provide rich, flexible desktop automation while preserving privacy and debuggability.
AI.method: Implement AnovaX as a single Python process that integrates a wake-word gate, speech recognition, and an LLM planner (Gemini) that outputs JSON tool-call plans. They add a whitelist/denylist safety layer, a multi-agent orchestrator that runs each plan step as typed child agents on a bounded thread pool, and an adaptive recovery loop that handles failures using a ReAct-style prompt and speculative execution of read-only tools. Each tool is represented as a specialized agent class (e.g., AppAgent, TypingAgent, BrowserAgent) with its own timeout, retry policy, and lock management. A recursive MetaAgent enables sub-goal delegation up to two levels. They also build a Flask-based remote interface that streams logs and the laptop’s screen to a phone over local WiFi and accepts remote commands.
AI.result: The resulting system can open and control desktop applications, type into them, browse and search, run concurrent actions via multiple agents, and recover from individual step failures. It can be fully operated from a phone over local WiFi, with real-time visualization of agent lifecycles and screen state, all while keeping the LLM away from direct keyboard control and keeping audio and actions local to the user’s machine.
AI.conclusion: A legible, few-thousand-line, fully local assistant architecture is sufficient to provide practical, concurrent desktop automation and remote control without relying on cloud pipelines or heavyweight assistant platforms. The architecture demonstrates that modular, agent-based planning plus explicit safety and recovery mechanisms can yield a powerful yet understandable system that respects user privacy and control.

title: Adaptive Multi-Step Lookahead Decoding for Diffusion Language Models
summary: Masked diffusion language models (DLMs) enable parallel text generation by iteratively refining masked tokens, offering a promising alternative to autoregressive decoding. Recent lookahead-based decoding methods improve the accuracy--efficiency trade-off by exploring future decoding states before committing token updates. However, existing approaches mainly rely on shallow one-step lookahead, which optimizes immediate information gain but can be suboptimal for longer-horizon decoding trajectories. Meanwhile, we find that a naive extension for deeper lookahead is also ineffective, as fixed-depth rollout introduces additional computation and cannot adapt to heterogeneous intermediate decoding states. Thus, in this work, we propose AdaLook, an adaptive lookahead framework for DLM decoding. AdaLook dynamically determines whether to continue rollout based on candidate-score variance and further enables branch expansion when intermediate rollout states require additional exploration. This design avoids unnecessary deep rollout while allowing the decoder to re-trigger lookahead from informative intermediate states. Experiments on various benchmarks and models demonstrate that AdaLook achieves a better accuracy--decoding steps trade-off than existing one-step lookahead decoding methods.

AI.tldr: Proposes AdaLook, an adaptive multi-step lookahead decoding framework for masked diffusion language models that improves the accuracy–efficiency trade-off over one-step lookahead methods.
AI.motivation: Masked diffusion language models can generate text in parallel by iteratively filling in masked tokens, but their decoding process must balance generation quality (accuracy) with efficiency (number of refinement steps). Existing lookahead-based decoders only perform shallow one-step lookahead, which optimizes short-term gains and can be suboptimal over longer decoding trajectories. Simply extending to fixed-depth multi-step lookahead is inefficient and fails to adapt to the varying uncertainty and difficulty of intermediate decoding states, motivating a more flexible and adaptive approach.
AI.method: Introduce AdaLook, an adaptive lookahead framework for decoding masked diffusion language models. Instead of using a fixed one-step or fixed-depth multi-step rollout, AdaLook monitors properties of intermediate rollout states—specifically, the variance of candidate token scores—to decide dynamically whether to continue looking further ahead or stop and commit updates. Additionally, when intermediate states exhibit high uncertainty or suggest multiple promising futures, AdaLook expands branches, exploring multiple rollouts selectively from those informative states. This adaptive control of rollout depth and branching tailors the computational budget to the structure and uncertainty of each decoding trajectory.
AI.result: On multiple benchmarks and with different masked diffusion language models, AdaLook yields better trade-offs between final generation accuracy (e.g., task performance or text quality metrics) and the number of decoding steps (or overall computational cost) compared to existing one-step lookahead decoding approaches. It achieves similar or higher accuracy with fewer steps, or higher accuracy under similar computational budgets.
AI.conclusion: Adaptive, variance-based control of lookahead depth and branch expansion in diffusion language model decoding can substantially improve the efficiency and effectiveness of parallel text generation. By avoiding unnecessary deep rollouts and selectively re-triggering lookahead from informative intermediate states, AdaLook outperforms fixed one-step lookahead schemes in the accuracy–efficiency trade-off, suggesting that adaptive search over decoding trajectories is a powerful tool for masked diffusion language models.

title: Precise but Uncoupled: Reviewer Precision Does Not Guarantee Critique Uptake in Multi-Agent Math Reasoning
comment: 48 pages, 20 figures, 25 tables. Public release website: https://huggingface.co/spaces/AgentsSci/scientific-agent-protocol-traces-site
summary: Many math- and science-oriented agent systems use hierarchical designs with specialized reviewer roles, assuming that a dedicated review stage should help turn wrong candidates into correct ones. We test this assumption on 4,181 verifier-grounded Omni-MATH problems using matched gpt-oss-120b actors. Collaboration adds little on the easiest tiers, but from tier 4 onward the gains open sharply; in this harder regime, broadcast-style peer discussion reaches higher final accuracy than a planner-executor-reviewer pipeline (PER). We ask whether this gap is explained by reviewer quality or by whether critique changes the next answer the protocol carries forward. It is not explained by reviewer precision alone: PER's reviewer is more precise than broadcast's (0.861 vs. 0.644), yet evaluator-verified useful critique is much less likely to change the next candidate and produces lower reviewer-guided repair. These results show that reviewer detection quality and critique uptake are empirically separable. Within matched PER interventions, forcing explicit acknowledgment lowers final accuracy, while embedding reviewer guidance directly in the solver's working context partially improves follow-through without closing the gap. Overall, reviewer-centric evaluation can overstate system quality: a protocol may spot errors well yet still fail to solve more problems if it does not act on those critiques.

AI.tldr: The paper empirically evaluates reviewer-based hierarchical agent designs on hard math problems and finds that high-quality reviewers do not guarantee better final problem-solving performance, mainly because their critiques often fail to change subsequent solutions.
AI.motivation: Many multi-agent and tool-using AI systems assume that adding a specialized reviewer or critic stage will systematically convert wrong answers into correct ones, but this assumption is rarely stress-tested, especially on hard math tasks. The authors want to rigorously examine how much reviewer roles actually help, and whether reviewer detection quality alone is a reliable signal of overall system effectiveness.
AI.method: They run large-scale experiments on 4,181 verifier-grounded Omni-MATH problems using matched gpt-oss-120b models as actors in different collaboration protocols. They compare a planner-executor-reviewer (PER) pipeline against a broadcast-style peer discussion setup across difficulty tiers. They analyze reviewer precision, how often critiques lead to changes in the next proposed solution, the success of reviewer-guided repairs, and the effect of different ways of incorporating reviewer feedback, including forced acknowledgment and embedding guidance in the solver’s context.
AI.result: Collaboration yields minimal gains on easy problems but large gains on harder tiers (tier 4+), where broadcast-style peer discussion significantly outperforms the PER pipeline in final accuracy. PER’s reviewer has substantially higher precision than the broadcast reviewer (0.861 vs. 0.644), yet PER’s critiques much less often alter the subsequent candidate answer and lead to weaker repairs. Reviewer detection quality and critique uptake behave as distinct factors. Forcing solvers to explicitly acknowledge reviewer comments reduces final accuracy, while injecting reviewer guidance into the solver’s working context somewhat improves adherence but still leaves a performance gap to broadcast discussion.
AI.conclusion: High-precision reviewer agents do not automatically translate into better overall problem solving; what matters is whether critiques are actually incorporated into subsequent reasoning. Reviewer quality and critique uptake are empirically separable, and systems can appear strong under reviewer-centric metrics while failing to solve more tasks. Designing collaborative agent protocols should therefore prioritize mechanisms for effective critique uptake, not just accurate error detection, and evaluation practices that focus only on reviewer quality may substantially overestimate system capability.

title: Better Starts, Better Ends: Bootstrapped Iterative Self-Reasoning Distillation for Compressed Reasoning
summary: Large reasoning models often solve problems through long chain-of-thought (CoT) traces, yet much of this computation is spent on redundant derivations, repeated self-verification, and detours that do not improve the final answer. Existing on-policy self-distillation methods reduce this cost by matching a student model to a concise copy of itself on prefixes sampled from the student's own rollouts. We show that this objective has an initialization bottleneck. Since supervision is applied only to visited prefixes, training from a verbose base model places the KL loss on contexts that are often noisy, redundant, or already off track. In such regions, a concise teacher can provide only local corrections, while the student continues to explore trajectories that an efficient reasoner should avoid. In this paper, we propose BIRD(Bootstrapped Iterative Self-Reasoning Distillation), a two-stage self-reasoning distillation method that improves the rollout distribution before on-policy training. BIRD first samples concise solutions from the base model under a brevity instruction, keeps only answer-correct traces, and performs a lightweight prompt-switch SFT step. The traces are generated with the brevity instruction but learned under the original task prompt, turning instruction-induced conciseness into a default reasoning behavior. Starting from this warm model, BIRD then applies on-policy reverse-KL distillation with a concise self-teacher, now on cleaner and more informative prefixes. Across Qwen3 series models, BIRD achieves a stronger accuracy-efficiency trade-off than prompting and cold-start on-policy distillation on MATH-500 and AIME benchmarks. On Qwen3-8B, it improves MATH-500 accuracy from 86.2% to 92.0% while reducing the average response length from 3,099 to 1,115 tokens. These results highlight prefix support as a central factor in efficient reasoning distillation.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces BIRD, a self-distillation method that makes large reasoning models shorter and more accurate by first fine-tuning on concise, correct traces and then applying on-policy distillation.
AI.motivation: Large reasoning models use long chain-of-thought traces with many redundant or unhelpful steps, wasting computation. Existing on-policy self-distillation tries to make models concise by imitating their own shorter traces, but this suffers from an initialization bottleneck because training focuses on noisy, off-track prefixes from a verbose base model. The authors want a method that improves both efficiency and accuracy by training on cleaner, more informative reasoning prefixes.
AI.method: BIRD (Bootstrapped Iterative Self-Reasoning Distillation) is a two-stage distillation approach. Stage 1: The base model is prompted with a brevity instruction to generate concise solutions; only answer-correct traces are kept, and a lightweight supervised fine-tuning (prompt-switch SFT) is done where the model is trained to reproduce these concise traces under the original task prompt without the brevity instruction. This converts instruction-induced brevity into default behavior. Stage 2: Starting from this warmed model, the authors run on-policy reverse-KL distillation with a concise self-teacher, now using prefixes sampled from the improved rollout distribution, so the loss is placed on cleaner, more informative contexts.
AI.result: On Qwen3 models, BIRD delivers a better accuracy–efficiency trade-off than both prompting with brevity instructions and standard cold-start on-policy distillation on MATH-500 and AIME benchmarks. For example, on Qwen3-8B, BIRD increases MATH-500 accuracy from 86.2% to 92.0% while cutting average response length from 3,099 tokens to 1,115 tokens.
AI.conclusion: BIRD overcomes the initialization bottleneck of on-policy self-distillation by first reshaping the rollout distribution toward concise, correct reasoning traces and then applying reverse-KL distillation. This two-stage process yields models that reason both more efficiently and more accurately, and it shows that having good prefix support—i.e., training on high-quality prefixes—is crucial for effective reasoning distillation.

title: DrawingVQA: A Real-World Benchmark for Multi-Depth Visual-Textual Reasoning on Construction Drawings
summary: We introduce DrawingVQA, the first benchmark designed to evaluate multimodal large language models (MLLMs) on real-world construction drawings -- a core media in architecture, civil, and many other engineering practices. Unlike natural images or schematic floor plans, construction drawings fuse abstract geometry, symbolic notation, tabular data, annotations, and domain-specific text, forming a uniquely complex visual-textual domain core to engineering workflows. DrawingVQA bridges this gap with 33 "Issued for Construction" drawings and 92 expertly curated question-answer pairs, spanning three reasoning depths: perceptual understanding, contextual interpretation, and domain-expert reasoning. To evaluate model capabilities, we present a dual categorization framework to jointly analyze performance across seven construction-engineering and four MLLM capability dimensions -- the first to explicitly map engineering workflows to AI reasoning competencies. Evaluations of state-of-the-art MLLMs reveal a substantial gap between model and expert performance, particularly at higher reasoning depths. This benchmark lays a foundation for domain-specialized multimodal reasoning to allow for advancement on integration of AI-driven understanding and real-world engineering workflows.

AI.tldr: DrawingVQA introduces the first benchmark to test multimodal large language models on complex real-world construction drawings, showing a large gap between current models and human experts, especially for deep engineering reasoning.
AI.motivation: Multimodal large language models are increasingly used on visual data, but existing benchmarks mainly focus on natural images or simple diagrams and do not reflect the complexity of construction drawings, which are central to real engineering workflows. There is a need to understand and measure how well AI systems can operate on this critical, highly structured visual-textual medium.
AI.method: The authors build DrawingVQA, a benchmark composed of 33 real, issued-for-construction drawings and 92 expert-written question–answer pairs. The questions are designed across three reasoning depths—perceptual, contextual, and domain-expert reasoning. They also define a dual categorization framework that classifies each question both by seven construction-engineering dimensions and four MLLM capability dimensions, explicitly linking engineering tasks to AI reasoning skills. They then evaluate several state-of-the-art MLLMs on this benchmark.
AI.result: State-of-the-art multimodal LLMs underperform human experts on DrawingVQA, with performance gaps that become especially severe at higher reasoning depths requiring domain-expert understanding and multi-step reasoning over drawings and associated text/notations.
AI.conclusion: DrawingVQA demonstrates that current MLLMs struggle with the complex, domain-specific reasoning required for real-world construction drawings. The benchmark, along with its dual categorization framework, provides a foundation and evaluation protocol for developing and measuring domain-specialized multimodal reasoning systems that are better aligned with engineering workflows.

title: Before the Action: Benchmarking LLMs on Prospective Hypothesis Discovery
summary: Large language models (LLMs) excel at answering pre-specified questions, yet their ability to navigate the open-ended, pre-conclusion stage of discovery remains largely unmeasured. We introduce Prospective Hypothesis Discovery (PHD), which asks models to autonomously construct grounded, discriminative, and testable hypothesis spaces from inconclusive evidence, including anomalous observations and fragmented records, to guide subsequent investigation. To evaluate this capability, we introduce HypoArena, comprising HypoData, a benchmark of 988 cases across six scientific and analytical domains, and HypoEval, an evaluation framework for open-ended hypothesis sets. To construct HypoData at scale, we propose Retrospective Context Regression, a Forge--Audit pipeline that reconstructs pre-conclusion contexts from completed expert documents by removing explicit conclusions, target hypotheses, and retrospective causal attributions while preserving the factual substrate. Because PHD admits multiple valid outputs, HypoEval combines bidirectional pairwise judgments with Bradley--Terry--Davidson aggregation for ranking and six-dimensional rubric scoring for diagnosis. Experiments on 15 frontier LLMs reveal clear capability stratification and model-dependent effects of structured analytical skills, with gains for several lower-performing models on HypoArena but regressions for other systems, including a top-performing model. Compared with absolute rubric scoring, arena evaluation resolves finer-grained differences among models, with aggregated rankings showing strong agreement with human experts and an independent judge. Together, these results support treating PHD as a distinct target for evaluating how LLMs formulate investigative directions when final conclusions are withheld. Our code and data are publicly available at github.com/SKYLENAGE-AI/HypoArena and github.com/SKYLENAGE-AI/HypoArena.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a new task and benchmark, Prospective Hypothesis Discovery (PHD) and HypoArena, to measure how well large language models generate plausible, testable hypotheses from incomplete evidence.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM evaluations mostly test question answering or inference after conclusions are known, not the earlier, exploratory phase where researchers must generate and prioritize hypotheses from messy, inconclusive data. The authors want a way to quantify and compare LLMs’ abilities in this open-ended, pre-conclusion discovery stage, which is crucial for scientific and analytical work.
AI.method: They define the Prospective Hypothesis Discovery (PHD) task: given inconclusive evidence (anomalies, partial records), models must propose grounded, discriminative, and testable hypothesis spaces. They build HypoArena, which has: (1) HypoData, a dataset of 988 cases from six domains, constructed using a Retrospective Context Regression pipeline that strips conclusions and causal attributions from expert documents while keeping factual context; and (2) HypoEval, an evaluation framework for open-ended hypothesis sets that uses bidirectional pairwise comparisons of model answers, Bradley–Terry–Davidson aggregation to derive rankings, and a six-dimensional rubric for diagnostic scoring. They run experiments on 15 advanced LLMs, including variants with structured analytical prompting, and analyze performance patterns.
AI.result: The experiments show clear stratification in PHD ability across 15 frontier LLMs. Adding structured analytical skills helps several weaker models on HypoArena but actually harms performance for some systems, including one of the top models. Arena-style pairwise evaluation is more sensitive than absolute rubric scoring, revealing finer distinctions between models. The aggregated rankings derived from HypoEval closely match human expert judgments and an independent judge, suggesting the framework is reliable.
AI.conclusion: Prospective Hypothesis Discovery is a distinct and meaningful capability of LLMs, separate from standard QA or reasoning tasks. HypoArena (HypoData + HypoEval) provides a scalable, reliable way to measure and analyze this ability. The findings imply that evaluating how models propose investigative directions under uncertainty is important for assessing their usefulness in scientific and analytical discovery, and that current models vary substantially on this dimension. The resources are publicly released for further research.

title: Do Coding Agents Need Executable World Models, Simplification, and Verification to Solve ARC-AGI-3?
summary: Our previous ARC-AGI-3 agent bundled executable world modeling, scheduled simplification, and exact replay verification, leaving unclear which idea accounted for its performance. We address this attribution question with four nested Codex-based agents: a textual baseline; a flexible-interface executable world model without replay verification; the same executable model with scheduled simplification; and a fixed-interface verification treatment that retains simplification and requires exact reproduction of recorded observations. The main study evaluates all four agents with gpt-5.4 and gpt-5.5 at high and xhigh reasoning effort on the public ARC-AGI-3 games. Exploratory follow-ups evaluate the textual and verification variants with gpt-5.6-sol at xhigh and max. The most robust result is that every agent variant improves with a stronger model and with greater reasoning effort. Within each model-effort setting, differences among variants are smaller than anticipated, while the effects of individual components vary across settings. Requiring a persistent executable deliverable is not universally beneficial: the textual variant outperforms the flexible-interface executable variant in both gpt-5.5 settings. Simplification improves performance in three of the four model-effort settings, with the weakest setting as the only exception. The complete verification treatment ranks first in all four settings, although it uses substantially more resources. In the gpt-5.6-sol follow-up, the verification variant fully solves every public game at both reasoning efforts, achieves about 99% RHAE, and uses fewer than half the total actions of the human baseline. Because the model postdates these games and held-out performance remains untested, this result should be interpreted as saturation of the public set only.

AI.tldr: They ablate the components of a prior ARC-AGI-3 agent using several nested Codex-based agent variants to see which design choices matter, finding that stronger models and more reasoning effort help most, simplification generally helps, and full verification with executable world models performs best but at higher cost, with newer models saturating the public benchmark.
AI.motivation: The earlier ARC-AGI-3 system combined several ideas—executable world modeling, scheduled simplification, and exact replay verification—so it was unclear which components were responsible for its strong performance. This paper aims to disentangle and quantify the contributions of these individual components and usage patterns, to better understand how to design effective agents for ARC-AGI-3 tasks and similar reasoning benchmarks.
AI.method: They construct four nested agent variants built on Codex-like models: (1) a pure textual baseline without executable world models; (2) a flexible-interface executable world model agent without replay verification; (3) the same executable agent augmented with scheduled simplification; and (4) a verification-focused agent that keeps simplification but uses a fixed interface and demands exact reproduction of logged observations. They evaluate all four agents using gpt-5.4 and gpt-5.5 under two reasoning-effort regimes (high and xhigh) on the public ARC-AGI-3 tasks, and run exploratory follow-ups on the textual and verification variants with a stronger model (gpt-5.6-sol) at higher effort levels (xhigh and max), measuring accuracy (including RHAE) and resource usage (e.g., number of actions).
AI.result: Across all settings, performance improves consistently with stronger base models and with higher reasoning effort. Differences between the four agent variants within a fixed model/effort configuration are smaller than expected, and the benefit of each architectural component (executable modeling, simplification, verification) depends on the specific setting. Surprisingly, enforcing a persistent executable artifact is not always advantageous: the textual-only agent beats the flexible executable one in both gpt-5.5 configurations. Scheduled simplification improves performance in three out of four model-effort settings, failing only in the weakest configuration. The full verification agent achieves the best results in all four main-study conditions but consumes substantially more compute/actions. In follow-up tests with gpt-5.6-sol, the verification agent solves every public ARC-AGI-3 game, reaches about 99% RHAE, and uses less than half as many actions as the human comparison group, though this is only demonstrated on the non-held-out public set.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that while architectural details like executable world models, simplification, and verification matter, the dominant performance drivers are underlying model strength and allocated reasoning budget. Executable-world requirements do not guarantee better results and can even hurt compared with a strong textual baseline in some regimes, whereas scheduled simplification usually helps and complete verification is consistently beneficial but resource-intensive. With a sufficiently strong model (gpt-5.6-sol), a verification-centric agent can essentially saturate the public ARC-AGI-3 benchmark using fewer actions than humans, but because the model was trained after the benchmark and no held-out evaluation is reported, this should be viewed as saturating the existing public set rather than as evidence of generally human-level ARC-AGI-3 competence.

title: Cost-efficient generative AI summarization for scalable automated essay scoring in educational assessment
summary: Automated essay scoring (AES) enables scalable assessment and timely feedback but remains challenged by transformer input-length limitations, which can cause information loss when processing long essays. This study proposes a generative AI-assisted summarization framework to improve long-form essay representation while maintaining scoring reliability. Using the ASAP 2.0 dataset, we generate controlled-length summaries with three GPT-5 variants (GPT-5, GPT-5 mini, and GPT-5 nano) and use them as inputs for downstream AES models. To preserve original writing signals, handcrafted linguistic features extracted from full essays are integrated with summary representations to form a hybrid framework. The approach is evaluated in terms of scoring performance, summarization quality, and computational cost. Scoring reliability is measured using quadratic weighted kappa (QWK), while summary quality is assessed through lexical overlap, semantic similarity, information retention, and redundancy metrics. Results show that GPT-5 mini achieves the highest agreement with human ratings, whereas GPT-5 produces the strongest summarization quality. Summary quality decreases for higher-scoring essays, indicating that more complex writing is more difficult to compress without information loss. These findings reveal trade-offs among model capacity, summary fidelity, cost efficiency, and preservation of educational constructs. This study provides an initial controlled evaluation of GPT-based summarization for AES and identifies important baselines and ablation studies required for future generalization. Overall, generative AI summarization offers a promising approach for scalable writing assessment while requiring careful validation of information preservation and fairness.

AI.tldr: The paper explores using GPT-5-based summarization to handle long essays in automated essay scoring, combining summaries with handcrafted features to avoid information loss and maintain scoring reliability.
AI.motivation: Transformer-based AES systems struggle with long essays due to input-length limits, leading to potential information loss and reduced scoring reliability. There is a need for a principled way to compress essays while preserving key educational constructs and writing signals, enabling scalable, timely assessment.
AI.method: Using the ASAP 2.0 dataset, the authors generate controlled-length summaries of essays with three GPT-5 variants (GPT-5, GPT-5 mini, GPT-5 nano). These summaries are fed into downstream AES models, and combined with handcrafted linguistic features extracted from the full essays to form a hybrid representation. They evaluate scoring reliability via quadratic weighted kappa and assess summary quality using lexical, semantic, information retention, and redundancy metrics, while also analyzing computational cost and conducting baseline/ablation-style comparisons across models and score ranges.
AI.result: GPT-5 mini yields the best alignment with human essay scores (highest QWK), while the larger GPT-5 model produces the best summary quality across overlap, similarity, and information retention metrics. However, summarization quality drops for higher-scoring, more complex essays, suggesting that these are harder to compress without losing important information. The experiments empirically surface trade-offs between model size, cost, summary fidelity, and preservation of educational constructs.
AI.conclusion: Generative AI-based summarization can effectively support automated scoring of long-form essays, offering a scalable alternative to processing full texts directly. However, due to trade-offs in model capacity, cost, and information preservation—especially for high-quality writing—the approach requires careful validation, including baseline and ablation studies, to ensure reliability, fairness, and generalizability in educational assessment contexts.

title: Beyond a Joke: Multi-Angle Reasoning for Detecting and Explaining Harmful Humor in Memes
summary: Internet memes intertwine visual cues, textual content, and cultural context, making them particularly challenging to interpret in scenarios where humor, sarcasm, and harmful intent coexist. These complexities highlight the need for explainable meme understanding systems that can provide reliable and structured reasoning to support both accurate classification and human interpretability. However, existing multimodal classifiers either overlook these interdependencies or provide only limited interpretability. In this paper, we introduce MAR-12, a novel framework that leverages Vision Language Models (VLMs) for meme detection and understanding in settings where humorous and hateful elements may coexist. The framework first interprets each meme through twelve structured perspectives derived from humor and hate theories. It then applies a role-aware soft-gated attention mechanism to learn how much each perspective should contribute, followed by a prototype-based classifier for the final prediction. Finally, explanations are synthesized using both perspective-specific reasoning and learned attention weights, ensuring transparent and context-grounded justifications. We evaluate MAR-12 on the PrideMM and Memotion datasets, where it achieves up to 80.3% accuracy for humor detection and 75.9% accuracy for hate detection, outperforming state-of-the-art approaches. Furthermore, both human and GPT-4-based evaluations confirm that MAR-12 produces coherent and persuasive explanations, particularly for memes in which humorous and harmful cues co-occur.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes MAR-12, an explainable vision-language framework that analyzes memes from 12 theory-driven perspectives to detect humor and hate, achieving state-of-the-art accuracy and producing human-evaluable textual explanations.
AI.motivation: Internet memes are multimodal and culturally loaded, often mixing humor, sarcasm, and potential harm, which makes automatic detection of humor and hate difficult and existing systems hard to interpret. There is a need for explainable models that not only classify memes accurately but also provide structured, theory-grounded reasoning that humans can understand and trust, especially when humorous and hateful elements coexist.
AI.method: The authors design MAR-12, a framework built on Vision Language Models (VLMs). For each meme, the system generates interpretations along 12 structured perspectives derived from established humor and hate theories. A role-aware soft-gated attention mechanism then learns how much each perspective should contribute to the decision. Finally, a prototype-based classifier uses the aggregated perspective representations to make predictions. Explanations are generated by combining the perspective-specific reasoning outputs with the learned attention weights, yielding transparent, context-grounded justifications for each decision.
AI.result: On the PrideMM and Memotion meme datasets, MAR-12 attains up to 80.3% accuracy for humor detection and 75.9% accuracy for hate detection, outperforming state-of-the-art baselines. Human annotators and GPT-4-based evaluators rate its explanations as coherent and persuasive, especially for challenging memes where humorous and harmful cues appear together.
AI.conclusion: MAR-12 effectively integrates theory-driven perspectives with VLMs and attention mechanisms to deliver both accurate and explainable meme understanding. Its superior performance and positively evaluated explanations suggest it is well-suited for analyzing memes that mix humor and hate, and it demonstrates a viable path toward more transparent and trustworthy multimodal content moderation systems.

title: CAMMAR: Culture-Aware Matryoshka for Metaphorical Arabic Representations
summary:
Metaphor in Arabic is a culturally grounded mechanism for constructing meaning, encoding cultural knowledge that shapes interpretation. Yet current Arabic language models typically collapse lexical, cultural, and metaphorical information into a single representational space, a phenomenon we term "semantic smearing". We introduce CAMMAR (Culture-Aware Matryoshka for Metaphorical Arabic Representations), a representation learning framework that organizes meaning into nested lexical, cultural, and metaphorical embedding subspaces through a staged semantic curriculum. The design implements compositional principles of Al-Jurjani's theory of nazum, modeling figurative meaning as compositionally grounded in prior semantic relations, and yields a training-free geometric measure of metaphoricity based on the distance between lexical and metaphorical representations.
  Evaluated on a new span-annotated Arabic metaphor set as word-matched figurative/literal pairs, the geometric readout detects metaphor well above chance when the inter-layer geometry is shaped by paired supervision (AUC up to 0.84; figurative outscores its literal counterpart for the same word in 82.6\% of pairs), but sits at chance under an unsupervised domain contrast alone, a clean separation between a legible-under-supervision regime and a non-emergent one.
