A controlled ablation shows that grounding the lexical layer in morphological roots gives a small but consistent gain, an effect absent from direct probing that reflects the layer's quality as a measurement anchor. We will release the datasets, cultural concept inventory, and code upon acceptance.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a new Arabic representation framework (CAMMAR) that separates lexical, cultural, and metaphorical meaning into nested embedding subspaces to better model metaphor and provide a geometric, training-free metaphoricity score.
AI.motivation: Existing Arabic language models blend lexical, cultural, and metaphorical information into a single semantic space, causing "semantic smearing" that obscures how culturally grounded metaphors are formed and interpreted. There is a need for representations that respect the compositional and cultural structure of metaphor in Arabic and allow explicit reasoning about metaphoricity.
AI.method: The authors design CAMMAR (Culture-Aware Matryoshka for Metaphorical Arabic Representations), a staged semantic curriculum that learns nested embedding layers for lexical, cultural, and metaphorical meaning, inspired by Al-Jurjani's theory of nazum. They define a geometric measure of metaphoricity as the distance between a word's lexical and metaphorical embeddings. They evaluate CAMMAR on a new span-annotated Arabic metaphor dataset with word-matched figurative/literal pairs, and conduct ablations, including grounding the lexical layer in morphological roots.
AI.result: When inter-layer geometry is trained with paired figurative/literal supervision, the geometric metaphoricity score detects metaphor well above chance (AUC up to 0.84; figurative embedding is more distant than literal for 82.6% of word pairs). Without such supervision, using only unsupervised domain contrast, the model performs at chance, showing that metaphor structure does not emerge automatically. Ablation reveals that using morphological roots to anchor the lexical layer provides a small but consistent performance gain, which is not visible via direct probing but improves the reliability of the geometric measure.
AI.conclusion: CAMMAR can structurally disentangle lexical, cultural, and metaphorical information in Arabic embeddings and supports a training-free geometric measure of metaphoricity that works well when guided by paired supervision. The findings highlight the importance of supervised structuring of semantic subspaces and of morphological grounding for using representation geometry as a robust measurement tool. The authors plan to release their datasets, cultural concept inventory, and code to support further research.

title: From Black Box to Executable Logic: Explainable Reinforcement Learning through Prolog Expert Systems
summary: A trained deep reinforcement learning policy is a black box, and we ask whether it can be made explainable by rewriting it as an executable logic program that reproduces its behaviour and that a person can read, a logic engine can run, and an optimizer can edit. We present a three-stage post-hoc transformation that extracts a frozen proximal policy optimization teacher, induces an ordered rule list from its decisions in the manner of classical relational learning, and emits the result as a Prolog program whose every decision is executed by an off-the-shelf logic engine; a subsequent expansion stage edits the rule base and accepts an edit only when policy evaluation certifies a return increase. We prove four guarantees. A return-loss bound makes the distilled program a machine-checkable certificate in a finite Markov decision process, and the expansion loop improves monotonically and terminates. For the continuous-observation setting we answer whether the conversion is possible at all: the propositional threshold instantiation converts the network to arbitrary fidelity as the resolution B grows, with disagreement O(1/B) and a return gap that closes at the same rate, and a matching lower bound shows the cost is exponential in the observation dimension for an oblique decision boundary. Empirically, on a two-room key-and-door task with 16,944 reachable states the expanded Prolog program attains exact optimal return in every seed and, in a budget-capped regime, exceeds the stochastic teacher on exact return in ten of ten seeds. On three continuous-control tasks the emitted program substitutes the network, matching the neural teacher within noise on Acrobot with eleven clauses and recovering about 97% of its return on CartPole, while on the finer-control LunarLander it recovers only partially, exactly the ceiling the exponential lower bound predicts.

AI.tldr: They transform deep RL policies into readable Prolog programs that closely match the original policy’s performance, with theoretical guarantees and empirical validation.
AI.motivation: Deep reinforcement learning policies are black boxes, making them hard to interpret, verify, and edit. The authors want a way to convert such neural policies into symbolic, executable logic programs that are understandable by humans, manipulable by optimizers, and runnable by standard logic engines, while preserving performance guarantees.
AI.method: They propose a three-stage post-hoc pipeline. First, they freeze a trained PPO neural network as a teacher policy. Second, they induce an ordered list of logical rules from the teacher’s decisions, using ideas from classical relational learning, and emit this as a Prolog program whose decisions are executed by an off-the-shelf logic engine. Third, they run an expansion/editing stage that iteratively modifies the rule base, accepting changes only if policy evaluation shows improved return. They also introduce a propositional threshold instantiation for continuous observations and analyze its approximation properties.
AI.result: They prove four guarantees: (1) a bound on return loss in finite MDPs, making the distilled program a machine-checkable performance certificate; (2) monotonic improvement of the expansion loop; (3) termination of that loop; and (4) for continuous observation spaces, that the threshold instantiation can approximate the neural policy to arbitrary fidelity as resolution B grows, with disagreement and return gap O(1/B), along with a matching exponential lower bound in observation dimension for oblique boundaries. Empirically, on a discrete two-room key-and-door task with 16,944 states, the expanded Prolog program reaches exact optimal return in all seeds and often surpasses the stochastic teacher under a budget cap. On three continuous-control benchmarks, the Prolog programs closely match the neural teacher on Acrobot with only eleven clauses, recover ~97% of return on CartPole, and partially recover performance on LunarLander, consistent with the theoretical exponential ceiling.
AI.conclusion: Deep RL policies can be systematically transformed into executable, human-readable logic programs that approximate or even improve on the original neural policies, with formal guarantees on performance loss and convergence. The approach scales well on simpler and moderately complex tasks but encounters fundamental, theoretically-predicted limitations in high-dimensional continuous-control settings with complex decision boundaries.

title: Contextual Semantic Relevance Tracks fMRI BOLD Responses During Naturalistic Speech Comprehension
summary: Naturalistic language comprehension requires listeners to process both local probabilistic expectations and contextual semantic relations. Surprisal has been widely used to quantify local word unexpectedness, but evidence that it robustly predicts fMRI BOLD responses during continuous comprehension has been mixed. This study investigates whether contextual semantic relevance, defined as how strongly an incoming word relates to its recent semantic context, predicts BOLD responses during naturalistic speech comprehension. We analyzed two public fMRI datasets, the Alice dataset and the Moth dataset, treating them as complementary rather than identical replications. Transformed BOLD responses were modeled with generalized additive mixed models (GAMMs) and original continuous BOLD time series were tested with FIR/deconvolution analyses. In Alice, semantic relevance was significant across all 12 ROIs (region of interest), whereas surprisal was not significant after FDR correction. In Moth, semantic relevance showed consistent negative effects across all 30 ROIs, while surprisal showed no comparable pattern. These findings suggest that semantic relevance is a promising BOLD-sensitive metric of contextual semantic fit. More broadly, our findings support the view that slow hemodynamic responses during naturalistic speech comprehension may be especially sensitive to contextual semantic integration, whereas local probabilistic prediction error may be more difficult to detect reliably with fMRI. In this sense, semantic relevance extends computational models of language comprehension from prediction alone toward context-sensitive semantic integration.

AI.tldr: The paper tests whether semantic relevance, rather than word surprisal, better explains fMRI BOLD responses during naturalistic speech comprehension, finding stronger and more consistent effects of semantic relevance across multiple brain regions and datasets.
AI.motivation: Previous work widely uses surprisal to quantify local word unexpectedness, but its ability to predict fMRI BOLD responses in naturalistic language comprehension has been inconsistent. There is a need to understand whether slower hemodynamic signals are more sensitive to broader contextual semantic relations rather than narrow probabilistic prediction error. The study aims to identify a neural metric that captures how well incoming words semantically fit their recent context during continuous speech.
AI.method: The authors analyzed two public fMRI datasets (Alice and Moth) of participants listening to continuous stories. They computed two predictors for each word: surprisal (local probabilistic unexpectedness) and semantic relevance (degree of semantic relatedness to the recent context). They modeled transformed BOLD responses using generalized additive mixed models (GAMMs) and validated results on the original continuous BOLD time series using FIR/deconvolution analyses, examining multiple predefined regions of interest (ROIs).
AI.result: In the Alice dataset, semantic relevance significantly predicted BOLD responses across all 12 ROIs, while surprisal failed to reach significance after FDR correction. In the Moth dataset, semantic relevance showed consistent negative effects across all 30 ROIs, indicating stronger BOLD responses for less semantically relevant words; surprisal showed no similarly consistent pattern. Across both datasets, semantic relevance outperformed surprisal as an explanatory variable for BOLD fluctuations during story listening.
AI.conclusion: Semantic relevance is a robust and BOLD-sensitive measure of contextual semantic fit during naturalistic speech comprehension, outperforming surprisal in explaining fMRI signals across datasets and brain regions. The findings support the idea that slow fMRI hemodynamic responses are particularly sensitive to context-driven semantic integration processes rather than to local prediction error alone. Conceptually, this shifts computational modeling of language comprehension from a primarily prediction-based framework toward one that explicitly incorporates context-sensitive semantic integration.

title: A Critical Analysis of Trustworthy AI Tools, Mark Frameworks, and the Implementation Chasms
summary: As artificial intelligence (AI) systems increasingly impact society, ensuring their ethical and trustworthy deployment has become a global priority. While a myriad of high-level ethical guidelines have emerged, criticism persists that these frameworks remain abstract and lack concrete mechanisms for implementation. This paper conducts a critical analysis of tools and trust mark frameworks intended to operationalize trustworthy AI (TAI), drawing on a comprehensive dataset from the OECD. Through empirical mapping and descriptive comparative analysis, we identify significant asymmetries in ethical focus, lifecycle coverage, stakeholder targeting, and tool typology. Our findings show a strong emphasis on fairness, transparency, and robustness, with comparatively little attention paid to explainability, digital security, and environmental sustainability. Moreover, most tools and certifications concentrate on post-development stages, with limited guidance for early design or data collection phases. Educational initiatives and policy engagement are notably underdeveloped, suggesting that current TAI efforts are dominated by technical and procedural measures within industry contexts. We argue that bridging the persistent chasm between AI principles and practice requires expanding ethical objectives, embedding ethics across the AI lifecycle, and fostering broader multi-stakeholder participation. This study provides both a diagnosis of existing implementation gaps and actionable recommendations for advancing more holistic, inclusive, and enforceable AI governance

AI.tldr: The paper critiques existing practical tools and trustmark schemes for "trustworthy AI" using OECD data, showing they are uneven, late-stage focused, and narrow in ethics coverage, and proposes ways to make AI governance more holistic, lifecycle-wide, and multi-stakeholder.
AI.motivation: High-level AI ethics principles are abundant but criticized as too abstract and hard to implement. The authors want to understand whether the emerging concrete tools and certification/trustmark frameworks truly translate these principles into practice, where the gaps are, and how to improve them.
AI.method: They empirically analyze an OECD dataset of tools and trustmark frameworks for trustworthy AI, using mapping and descriptive comparative analysis to examine their ethical focus, AI lifecycle coverage, target stakeholders, and tool types.
AI.result: They find strong focus on fairness, transparency, and robustness, but weak coverage of explainability, digital security, and environmental sustainability. Most tools address post-development phases rather than early design and data collection. Educational and policy-engagement tools are scarce, and current efforts are mainly technical/procedural and industry-centric.
AI.conclusion: To close the gap between AI ethics principles and real-world practice, AI governance must broaden its ethical aims, embed ethics throughout the entire AI lifecycle, and involve a wider range of stakeholders. The paper offers a diagnosis of current implementation gaps and concrete recommendations for more holistic, inclusive, and enforceable trustworthy AI frameworks.

title: Conditional Reliability of Toxicity Signals for Multilingual and Code-Mixed Abuse Detection
summary: Moderation systems increasingly rely on external toxicity tools, but those tools are unreliable under code-mixing, transliteration, slang, and language mismatch. We study the \emph{conditional reliability} of toxicity priors in Indian multilingual and code-mixed short text: English toxicity, Indic abuse, and rule-based severity cues can be useful evidence, but only in some linguistic and abuse-severity contexts. We propose ToxGate, a trust-fusion head that conditions each auxiliary signal on the encoder representation before adding it to the prediction state. Across three short-text abuse datasets, four transformer encoders, and five seeds per setting, ToxGate improves over matched plain encoders in 10 of 12 in-domain settings and 7 of 8 transfer settings. The largest and most interpretable gains occur in high-risk moderation slices, including explicit slurs, violent threats, and cross-dataset transfer. The broader lesson is that moderation systems should treat external toxicity tools and priors as conditional evidence rather than fixed features or ground truth, in focused ablations, source-specific gating gives the strongest results in transfer, severe-abuse slices, and high-risk triage.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces ToxGate, a model component that selectively trusts external toxicity tools for detecting abuse in Indian multilingual and code-mixed short texts, improving moderation reliability, especially in high-risk cases.
AI.motivation: Existing toxicity and abuse-detection tools perform poorly on Indian multilingual and code-mixed text, especially with transliteration, slang, and language mismatches. However, these external tools still contain useful information if used carefully. The authors are motivated to understand when and how to rely on such noisy toxicity priors rather than treating them as universally valid features or ground truth, aiming to improve moderation accuracy and safety in complex linguistic settings.
AI.method: The authors formalize the idea of conditional reliability of toxicity priors and propose ToxGate, a trust-fusion head added on top of transformer encoders. ToxGate takes auxiliary signals—English toxicity scores, Indic abuse indicators, and rule-based severity cues—and conditions each of them on the encoder’s internal representation before integrating them into the prediction state. This gating mechanism effectively learns when each external signal should be upweighted or downweighted based on the linguistic and abuse-severity context. They evaluate ToxGate across three Indian multilingual/code-mixed short-text abuse datasets, four transformer encoders, and multiple random seeds, comparing against plain encoder baselines without gated priors. They also conduct in-domain and cross-dataset transfer experiments and analyze performance on high-risk moderation slices (e.g., explicit slurs, violent threats).
AI.result: ToxGate outperforms matched plain transformer encoders in 10 out of 12 in-domain scenarios and 7 out of 8 transfer scenarios. The largest and clearest gains appear in high-risk moderation contexts, such as explicit slurs, violent threats, and cross-dataset transfer cases, where conditional use of external priors helps most. Source-specific gating and focused ablations show that selectively trusting individual external sources yields the strongest improvements, particularly in transfer, severe-abuse subsets, and high-risk triage settings.
AI.conclusion: External toxicity tools and priors should not be treated as fixed features or pseudo–ground truth in moderation systems, especially for multilingual and code-mixed content. Instead, they should be modeled as conditional evidence whose usefulness depends on context. The proposed ToxGate trust-fusion head demonstrates that conditioning each auxiliary signal on the encoder’s internal representation leads to more reliable abuse detection, with especially strong benefits for severe and high-risk content and for cross-dataset transfer. The broader takeaway is that moderation architectures should explicitly learn when to trust or ignore each external prior through source-specific gating mechanisms.

title: Logic, Optimization, and Artificial Intelligence
summary: Logic and optimization can, in combination, make valuable contributions to rule-based AI. Logic is the obvious medium for encoding a rule base and drawing inferences from it, while optimization provides a powerful technology for computing inferences. Their combination has taken on new relevance amid a growing concern for transparency in AI. which is important for reproducibility, explainability, trustworthiness, and fairness. Rule-based AI provides a natural solution to transparency that is becoming increasingly practical due to today's highly advanced optimization methods. This article surveys several areas of logic-optimization partnership, including probabilistic logic, Bayesian logic, belief logics and Dempster-Shafer theory, nonmonotonic (default) logic, many-valued logics, and inference of logical formulas from noisy data based on Boolean regression. It shows how to compute projections, the fundamental problem of both logic and optimization, using decision diagrams and logic-based Benders decomposition. It describes the use of postoptimality analysis to explain how conclusions are reached, further enhancing transparency, as well as the role of optimization in answer set programming modulo theories. The paper concludes by suggesting possible future research directions.

AI.tldr: Survey of how combining logic with optimization improves transparent, rule-based AI and directions for future work.
AI.motivation: There is growing concern that AI systems should be transparent for reasons such as reproducibility, explainability, trustworthiness, and fairness. Rule-based AI, grounded in logic, is naturally interpretable but can be computationally demanding. Optimization techniques are powerful for computation but are often viewed as black-box. The paper is motivated by the idea that integrating logic with optimization can yield AI systems that are both efficient and transparent, addressing modern demands on responsible AI.
AI.method: The paper takes the form of a survey and conceptual synthesis rather than a single new algorithm. It systematically reviews multiple strands of research where logic and optimization interact: probabilistic and Bayesian logics, belief logics and Dempster-Shafer theory, nonmonotonic (default) logics, many-valued logics, and Boolean regression for learning logical formulas from noisy data. It then discusses concrete techniques for computing logical projections using decision diagrams and logic-based Benders decomposition, and examines how postoptimality analysis and optimization can be used to generate explanations and support answer set programming modulo theories.
AI.result: The authors unify diverse research themes under the umbrella of a logic–optimization partnership, showing that many logical inference tasks can be cast as optimization problems and efficiently solved with modern optimization technology. They demonstrate that computing projections—a core operation in both logic and optimization—can be effectively handled via decision diagrams and logic-based Benders decomposition. They also identify how postoptimality analysis can be exploited to trace and explain inferences, and they clarify the role of optimization in answer set programming modulo theories.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that combining logic with optimization yields a promising pathway to scalable, transparent rule-based AI systems. It emphasizes that optimization not only accelerates logical inference but also supports explanation through postoptimality analysis. The survey highlights gaps and open challenges, and proposes future research directions on deeper integration of logical formalisms with advanced optimization methods to further improve transparency, efficiency, and applicability of AI in complex, data-rich settings.

title: An MLIR-Based Compilation Method for Large Language Models
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) have become the dominant workload on modern AI accelerators, yet deploying them on specialized hardware still faces two core challenges: how to import a trained model into a compiler-friendly intermediate representation, and how to efficiently schedule the autoregressive inference loop under limited on-chip memory. This paper presents an MLIR (Multi-Level Intermediate Representation) based compilation method for large language models, illustrated using two dialects of operators, TopOp and TpuOp. TopOp serves as a high-level graph dialect that is independent of both the source framework and the target chip, and is responsible for expressing model semantics; TpuOp serves as the target hardware dialect, carrying chip-related decisions such as quantization, layer groups, and memory layout. A model is first represented as TopOp, then lowered layer by layer to TpuOp, and finally a deployable binary is generated. In addition, each Transformer layer is split into three stages for static compilation: prefill, prefill_kv (prefill with historical key-value cache), and decode, so as to accommodate the different computational characteristics of prompt-parallel processing and per-token generation. The method has been implemented in the TPU-MLIR compiler{https://github.com/sophgo/tpu-mlir} and the LLM-TPU deployment project\footnote{https://github.com/sophgo/LLM-TPU}, supporting a variety of generative models including the Qwen, Llama, InternVL, and MiniCPM-V series, as well as multiple quantization and deployment forms such as GPTQ, AWQ, and AutoRound.

AI.tldr: An MLIR-based compilation flow for deploying LLMs on specialized TPUs, using a high-level, hardware-agnostic dialect (TopOp) and a hardware-specific dialect (TpuOp), plus a three-stage decomposition of Transformer layers to efficiently support both prefill and token-by-token decoding under tight on-chip memory constraints.
AI.motivation: LLMs are now the main workload on AI accelerators, but two major obstacles hinder efficient deployment on custom chips: (1) models trained in diverse frameworks must be converted into a compiler-friendly IR while preserving semantics, and (2) autoregressive inference involves very different computation and memory patterns for prompt processing vs. per-token decoding, which is hard to schedule on accelerators with limited on-chip memory. A systematic compilation and scheduling approach is needed to bridge the gap between framework models and hardware execution for various LLMs and quantization schemes.
AI.method: The paper proposes an MLIR-based compilation pipeline with two custom dialects. TopOp is a high-level, framework-agnostic and hardware-agnostic graph dialect that encodes the model’s semantic structure. TpuOp is a hardware-specific dialect that encodes chip-related implementation decisions such as quantization, operator grouping, and memory layout. The pipeline first imports the model as TopOp, then lowers it layer-by-layer to TpuOp, and finally emits deployable binaries. For LLMs, each Transformer layer is split into three statically compiled stages—prefill, prefill_kv (prefill with existing key/value cache), and decode—to match the distinct compute/memory characteristics of prompt-parallel and per-token phases and to better manage on-chip memory. The solution is implemented on the TPU-MLIR compiler and integrated into the LLM-TPU deployment project.
AI.result: The method has been implemented in the open-source TPU-MLIR compiler and the LLM-TPU deployment stack. It supports multiple families of LLMs (e.g., Qwen, Llama, InternVL, MiniCPM-V) and various quantization/deployment schemes (GPTQ, AWQ, AutoRound), demonstrating that the proposed IR design and compilation strategy generalize across model architectures and compression methods on TPUs.
AI.conclusion: An MLIR-based, two-dialect compilation framework combined with a three-stage decomposition of Transformer layers effectively addresses key challenges of deploying LLMs on specialized accelerators: model import into a suitable IR and efficient scheduling of autoregressive inference under memory constraints. The demonstrated support for several LLM families and quantization schemes suggests that this approach is practical, extensible, and applicable to real-world LLM deployment pipelines on TPUs.

title: SeerGuard: A Safety Framework for Mobile GUI Agents via World Model Prediction
summary: Mobile graphical user interface (GUI) agents have demonstrated remarkable capabilities in automating complex tasks, yet they introduce critical safety risks where a single erroneous action can lead to irreversible consequences. Existing safety mechanisms are primarily reactive, lacking the ability to assess risks before execution. In this paper, we introduce SeerGuard, a consequence-aware safety framework designed to mitigate these risks through pre-execution instruction-level screening and action-level risk assessment. Specifically, the action-level assessment analyzes agent-proposed actions within current GUI states, anticipating likely outcomes to identify risks before they are executed. To enable these capabilities, we construct a unified safety-augmented world model (SAWM) via multi-task learning, integrating semantic next-state prediction with safety risk assessment. Extensive experiments demonstrate that SeerGuard generalizes effectively across diverse mobile GUI agents. On Qwen3-VL-8B-Instruct, it increases the safety-utility score from $0.191$ to $0.596$ at $ω=0.8$ and reduces the risk-cost score from $0.347$ to $0.130$ at $α=0.8$. Further analyses on our SAWM validate the effectiveness of the instruction-level screening, alongside the capability of action risk assessment and next-state prediction.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes SeerGuard, a safety framework for mobile GUI agents that predicts and prevents risky actions before execution using a unified safety-augmented world model.
AI.motivation: Mobile GUI agents can autonomously operate apps and perform complex tasks, but a single wrong tap or swipe can trigger irreversible actions such as financial loss, data deletion, or privacy breaches. Current safety mechanisms mostly react after the fact or only block obviously unsafe instructions, and they lack the ability to reason about the concrete consequences of low-level GUI actions in a given state. This gap motivates a proactive, consequence-aware safety mechanism that can evaluate both user instructions and the agent’s proposed actions before they are executed, and that can generalize across different agents and tasks.
AI.method: The authors introduce SeerGuard, a two-level safety framework for mobile GUI agents. First, it performs pre-execution instruction-level screening to filter or flag risky user instructions. Second, it conducts action-level risk assessment by analyzing the agent’s proposed GUI actions in the current screen state and predicting likely next states and outcomes. To support both tasks, they build a Safety-Augmented World Model (SAWM) via multi-task learning, jointly training it to perform semantic next-state prediction and safety risk assessment. SAWM takes the current GUI state and proposed action as input and outputs both the predicted semantic effect on the UI and a risk evaluation, enabling SeerGuard to intervene before the agent executes the action. They evaluate SeerGuard across multiple mobile GUI agents, including Qwen3-VL-8B-Instruct as a backbone.
AI.result: In experiments, SeerGuard improves both safety and utility across diverse mobile GUI agents. When integrated with Qwen3-VL-8B-Instruct, it raises the safety-utility score from 0.191 to 0.596 at ω=0.8, indicating substantially better task completion under safety constraints, and it decreases the risk-cost score from 0.347 to 0.130 at α=0.8, showing fewer or less severe unsafe events. Additional analyses of the Safety-Augmented World Model demonstrate that instruction-level screening is effective, and that the model can accurately assess the risk of proposed actions and predict likely next GUI states.
AI.conclusion: SeerGuard provides a proactive, consequence-aware safety layer for mobile GUI agents by combining instruction-level screening with action-level risk assessment built on a unified Safety-Augmented World Model. The framework can anticipate the consequences of GUI actions in context and intervene before risky actions are executed, improving safety while maintaining or enhancing task utility. Empirical results show that SeerGuard generalizes across agents and significantly reduces risk without severely limiting capabilities, supporting its use as a general safety mechanism for mobile GUI automation.

title: How Much Human Label Variation Does Formal Semantic Structure Explain?: Group-Level Effects and Item-Level Ceilings in NLI
comment: 10 pages, 1 figure. Code and preregistered analysis log: https://github.com/oudeis01/nli-hlv-structure
summary: Human label variation in natural language inference is increasingly treated as signal rather than noise, but how much of it formal semantic structure explains has not been measured directly. We measure it on the 3,113 SNLI and MNLI items of ChaosNLI, using a rule-based operator and monotonicity tagger validated against MED (0.883 agreement at the edit site, 0.807 on the sentence-level summary our analyses consume), three preregistered analysis blocks, and full reporting of negative results. Three bounds emerge. First, a group-level boundary: hypotheses that are not purely upward monotone show reliably higher label entropy (Cliff's delta = -0.284), and rank-based tests defend the effect against operator-presence and length reductions, though a bounded-outcome sensitivity check weakens the regression form of the length defense. Second, an item-level ceiling: the same formal profiles explain only 3.3 to 3.6 percent of entropy variance and reach a median-split AUC of 0.606, too weak to identify high-disagreement items. Third, composition invariance: across the boundary, three high-powered preregistered contrasts on validated error shares and explanation-type shares (VariErr, LiTEx) all return null results. In this sample, formal semantic structure shifts how much annotators disagree by a small amount and does not detectably change what they disagree about. ChaosNLI-S/M consists of items selected for low original agreement, and every claim is conditioned on that scope. All analyses were preregistered in a version-controlled research log, whose audit trail, including one corrected interpretation rule, the paper discloses.

AI.tldr: The paper tests how much formal semantic structure (monotonicity and logical operators) can explain variation in human labels for NLI, and finds only a small effect on how much people disagree and no detectable effect on what they disagree about.
AI.motivation: Recent work treats disagreement among human annotators in NLI datasets as meaningful signal, not just noise, but it is unclear how much of this disagreement is driven by formal semantic properties of the sentences, such as monotonicity and logical operators. The authors want to quantify the contribution of formal semantics to label entropy and types of disagreement on a well-known NLI benchmark subset (ChaosNLI).
AI.method: They use a rule-based operator and monotonicity tagger, validated on the MED dataset with reasonably high agreement, to annotate the 3,113 SNLI and MNLI items in ChaosNLI. They then run three preregistered blocks of statistical analyses: (1) group-level comparisons of label entropy between upward-monotone vs. non‑purely‑upward‑monotone hypotheses, including controls for operator presence and length; (2) item-level regressions and classification (AUC) to see how much variance in entropy can be explained by formal profiles; and (3) tests of whether moving across the monotonicity “boundary” changes error-type or explanation-type distributions (using VariErr and LiTEx), all with full negative-result reporting and sensitivity checks.
AI.result: They find three main empirical bounds: (1) At the group level, hypotheses that are not purely upward monotone systematically have higher label entropy, with a medium-small effect size (Cliff’s delta = -0.284), and this holds under rank-based tests that account for operator presence and length, though a sensitivity check weakens the regression-based length control. (2) At the item level, formal semantic profiles explain only 3.3–3.6% of the variance in label entropy and yield a median-split AUC of 0.606, which is too low to reliably flag high-disagreement items. (3) Regarding composition, there is no detectable difference across the monotonicity boundary in the share of error types or explanation types: three preregistered, high-powered contrasts on validated error and explanation labels all return null results.
AI.conclusion: Formal semantic structure, as captured by their monotonicity and operator annotations, has a statistically reliable but small impact on how much annotators disagree in ChaosNLI-S/M and does not measurably affect what they disagree about. Thus, semantic monotonicity is not a strong driver of item-level disagreement patterns in these NLI datasets, and other factors must account for most of the observed variation. The study also underscores the value of preregistration, validation of rule-based tools, and transparent audit trails when probing the origins of annotator disagreement.

title: MGDT: MLLM-Guided Diffusion Transformer with Relation-Adaptive Mixture-of-Experts for Multimodal Knowledge Graph Completion
summary: Multimodal Knowledge Graph Completion (MKGC) requires inferring missing entities from structural, textual, and visual cues. Existing diffusion-based MKGC methods usually denoise directly on raw multimodal features. Such a design forces the denoiser to simultaneously perform relation-dependent cue selection, cross-modal semantic alignment, and structure-aware entity generation, which introduces noisy and semantically inconsistent conditions for diffusion and consequently leads to suboptimal completion performance. To address this limitation, we propose MGDT: MLLM-Guided Diffusion Transformer with Relation-Adaptive Mixture-of-Experts (MGDT), a novel MKGC framework built on an align-then-diffuse paradigm. MGDT first employs a Relation-Adaptive Semantic Routing Mixture-of-Experts (RASR-MoE) module to select relation-relevant multimodal semantic transformation paths and suppress irrelevant modality interference. MGDT then uses a frozen Multimodal Large Language Model (MLLM) as a semantic anchor to align the routed multimodal representations into a unified latent space and reduce cross-modal semantic heterogeneity. Finally, a Knowledge Graph Diffusion Transformer (KGDT) performs graph-conditioned denoising generation in the aligned space to produce the missing entity representation. Experiments on three benchmark datasets show that MGDT consistently outperforms strong baselines.

AI.tldr: They propose a new multimodal knowledge graph completion framework, MGDT, that first aligns multimodal semantics with relation-aware routing and an MLLM, then performs diffusion-based generation in this aligned space, achieving state-of-the-art results.
AI.motivation: Existing diffusion-based multimodal KGC models directly denoise on raw multimodal features (text, vision, structure). This forces a single denoising process to handle relation-dependent cue selection, cross-modal alignment, and entity generation simultaneously. As a result, the diffusion process is conditioned on noisy, semantically misaligned signals, which harms completion accuracy. The authors want to decouple and structure these steps to improve performance and stability.
AI.method: They introduce MGDT, based on an "align-then-diffuse" paradigm. (1) A Relation-Adaptive Semantic Routing Mixture-of-Experts (RASR-MoE) module dynamically selects relation-relevant semantic transformation paths over multimodal inputs, suppressing irrelevant modalities. (2) A frozen Multimodal Large Language Model (MLLM) acts as a semantic anchor to align the routed multimodal features into a unified latent space, reducing cross-modal heterogeneity. (3) A Knowledge Graph Diffusion Transformer (KGDT) performs graph-conditioned denoising in this aligned latent space to generate the representation of the missing entity.
AI.result: On three standard MKGC benchmarks, MGDT consistently surpasses strong diffusion-based and other baseline models, indicating that the align-then-diffuse design and relation-adaptive MoE routing improve multimodal KG completion performance.
AI.conclusion: Separating semantic alignment from diffusion via an align-then-diffuse pipeline, combined with relation-adaptive MoE routing and MLLM-based semantic anchoring, yields more coherent diffusion conditions and better multimodal KG completion. MGDT demonstrates that leveraging frozen MLLMs as alignment anchors and designing relation-aware expert routing are effective strategies for improving MKGC systems.

title: DECODEM: Data Extraction from Corporate Organizational Documents via Enhanced Methods
summary:
Much empirical legal research depends on translating unstructured text into structured variables. In corporate governance research as elsewhere, this translation has traditionally relied on human coding of documents such as charters and bylaws, a process that is costly, difficult to scale, and often opaque. This paper introduces DECODEM, a set of benchmark datasets for evaluating the automated extraction of corporate governance variables from organizational documents. The benchmarks pair randomly sampled corporate charters and bylaws with high-quality human annotations covering a range of governance provisions commonly studied in empirical work.
  Using these datasets, the paper evaluates several large-language-model extraction pipelines that vary in prompt design, task decomposition, and document handling. The underlying task consists of a set of document-level binary classification problems, one for each governance variable. The results show that automated extraction is feasible at a high level of accuracy for many provisions, with median performance near the upper bound across approaches. At the same time, performance varies systematically across variables, with a small number of provisions accounting for most of the remaining errors. More elaborate prompting strategies and cascading pipelines do not consistently improve performance for frontier models, but substantially narrow the gap between frontier and efficiency-oriented models in some settings, suggesting that pipeline design can partly substitute for model capability.
  By providing a standardized benchmark and a systematic evaluation of extraction methods, the paper demonstrates that current frontier models can extract legally meaningful information from complex corporate documents with high accuracy and suggests an important future role for automated feature extraction in constructing corporate governance datasets.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces DECODEM, benchmark datasets and evaluations showing that large language models can accurately extract corporate governance variables from complex legal documents, highlighting where they succeed and where they still struggle.
AI.motivation: Empirical legal and corporate governance research requires converting unstructured legal documents (charters, bylaws) into structured variables, but traditional human coding is costly, hard to scale, and opaque. There is no standard benchmark to rigorously test automated extraction methods, especially using modern large language models, so researchers lack reliable guidance on whether and how to automate this process.
AI.method: The authors construct DECODEM, benchmark datasets consisting of randomly sampled corporate charters and bylaws paired with high-quality human annotations for many governance provisions of empirical interest. They then design and compare multiple LLM-based extraction pipelines for document-level binary classification of each governance variable. Pipelines vary in prompt design, task decomposition, and document handling (e.g., different ways of chunking or presenting documents). They systematically evaluate accuracy across variables and pipelines, and compare performance of frontier versus efficiency-oriented models.
AI.result: Automated extraction of governance variables from corporate charters and bylaws is feasible with high accuracy for many provisions, with median performance close to an estimated upper bound. However, accuracy is heterogeneous across variables: a small subset of difficult provisions causes most residual errors. More complex prompting and cascading pipelines do not reliably improve accuracy for the strongest (frontier) models, but can significantly improve the performance of smaller, efficiency-focused models, thereby reducing the gap between them and frontier models in some cases.
AI.conclusion: DECODEM establishes a standardized benchmark for automated extraction of corporate governance variables and shows that current frontier LLMs can reliably extract legally meaningful features from complex corporate documents. While some specific provisions remain challenging, the findings support the use of LLM-based extraction in constructing corporate governance datasets and indicate that thoughtful pipeline design can partially compensate for weaker model capabilities. This paves the way for more scalable, transparent, and replicable empirical legal research based on automated feature extraction.

title: Neuro-Symbolic AI for LEED compliance: Document-Centric Benchmarking, Deterministic Numeric Checking, and When Multimodal Hurts
summary: LEED v4.1 BD+C certification remains a document-intensive process that requires reviewers to read hundreds of pages of project evidence and apply credit-specific threshold logic by hand. This paper investigates whether small, locally deployed language models can perform meaningful screening of LEED documentation and how deterministic symbolic components should share that work. A neuro-symbolic pipeline is introduced that aligns project PDFs to LEED credit sections, retrieves evidence with credit-aware keyword signatures, verifies compliance with a locally hosted 4-billion-parameter language model, and applies a LEED-specific numeric checker to quantitative thresholds. Experiments on four university buildings (484 PDFs, 153 credit-level decisions) show that a 4-billion-parameter model (gemma3:4b) is the strongest text-only core verifier, achieving 67.3% accuracy and outperforming a larger 8-billion-parameter model (llama3.1:8b) in this task. The deterministic numeric checker corrects arithmetic errors on key quantitative credits, moving EA-p2 from 50% to 100% accuracy and improving several other credits when required values are reliably extracted. At the same time, the full neuro-symbolic configuration achieves 61.6% overall accuracy, trailing the best text-only baseline due to extraction failures and conservative behavior on qualitative categories. Systematic ablations show that adding low-resolution drawing images (150-300 dpi) consistently reduces accuracy, and that prompt effectiveness depends on the building's ground-truth PASS rate: rubric prompts perform best on documentation-rich projects, while chain-of-thought prompts perform best on documentation-lean projects. Within the specific scope of LEED v4.1 BD+C compliance verification over raw project documentation, this pipeline and its baselines provide an initial reproducible reference point for both accuracy and failure modes.

AI.tldr: A neuro-symbolic pipeline using a small local language model and deterministic checkers is proposed to semi-automate LEED v4.1 BD+C documentation review; it reaches moderate accuracy, clarifies where symbolic checks help, and establishes a reproducible baseline and failure analysis for this specialized task.
AI.motivation: LEED certification review is laborious and document-heavy, requiring human experts to manually read large volumes of PDFs and apply complex, credit-specific rules. There is interest in automating or assisting this process, but constraints on data privacy and deployment favor small, locally hosted models. The authors want to understand whether such models, combined with symbolic logic, can meaningfully screen LEED documentation, how work should be divided between neural and symbolic components, and what performance and failure modes emerge in realistic building projects.
AI.method: The authors design a neuro-symbolic pipeline for LEED v4.1 BD+C compliance checking. First, project PDFs are aligned to LEED credit sections. Then a retrieval stage uses credit-aware keyword signatures to extract potentially relevant evidence. A locally hosted 4B-parameter language model (and baselines including an 8B model) serves as the core verifier, taking retrieved text and predicting credit compliance. For quantitative credits, a LEED-specific numeric checker applies explicit threshold logic to the values extracted by the model, correcting arithmetic and threshold errors. Experiments are conducted on four university building projects comprising 484 PDFs and 153 credit-level decisions, with multiple ablations: text-only versus text+low-res drawings, different prompting strategies (rubric vs. chain-of-thought), and with/without the numeric checker.
AI.result: The 4B-parameter gemma3:4b model is the best text-only verifier, achieving 67.3% accuracy and outperforming a larger 8B-parameter llama3.1:8b on this specialized task. The deterministic numeric checker substantially improves performance on quantitative credits, e.g., raising the EA-p2 prerequisite from 50% to 100% accuracy and boosting several other credits when value extraction succeeds. However, the fully integrated neuro-symbolic system attains 61.6% overall accuracy, worse than the best text-only baseline, primarily due to extraction failures and cautious behavior on qualitative assessments. Ablation studies show that adding low-resolution drawings (150–300 dpi) systematically reduces accuracy, and that ideal prompting strategy depends on the underlying PASS rate of a project: rubric-style prompts perform best for documentation-rich buildings, while chain-of-thought prompts work better for documentation-lean ones.
AI.conclusion: Small, locally deployable language models, when combined with symbolic checkers, can provide useful but imperfect automation for LEED v4.1 BD+C documentation review. The neuro-symbolic pipeline offers a clear, reproducible benchmark of achievable accuracy and typical failure patterns on real project data. Symbolic numeric checking is particularly beneficial for quantitative credits, while qualitative and extraction-related challenges remain key bottlenecks. Image inputs in the form of low-res drawings are not helpful in the current setup, and prompting strategies should be tailored to project documentation characteristics. The work is framed as an initial step and reference point rather than a complete replacement for human LEED reviewers.

title: Induction in Both Directions: A Mechanistic Analysis of In-Context Learning in Masked Diffusion Language Models
summary: While the internal mechanisms of autoregressive (AR) transformers have been studied extensively, much less is known about diffusion language models (DLMs), an emerging alternative that generates text by iterative denoising. In this work, we study how DLMs implement induction, a mechanism behind in-context learning in which the model finds a repeated context and copies the token that followed it. Our analysis compares attention-only AR models and absorbing-mask DLMs with matched architectures. We find that DLMs learn a bidirectional induction circuit, where previous-token and next-token heads write local context into the residual stream and later induction heads use it to find and copy the answer from the matching source position. The circuit is direction-symmetric, working whether the source appears in the past or in the future. When only left context is visible, matching what an AR model sees, the DLM does not outperform its AR counterpart in induction capabilities. However, we observe it has stronger induction when both sides of the masked token are visible, pointing to bidirectional context access rather than a stronger one-sided mechanism. Beyond induction, we provide causal evidence that DLMs compute the global fraction of masked tokens and use it as an implicit timestep, even though they are given no explicit timestep embedding.

AI.tldr: The paper analyzes how diffusion language models (DLMs) implement induction (a key in-context learning mechanism) and compares them to autoregressive (AR) transformers with matched architectures.
AI.motivation: Although AR transformers have been mechanistically studied in depth, the internal workings of diffusion language models, which generate text via iterative denoising, remain poorly understood. In particular, it is unclear how DLMs realize induction, the mechanism that detects repeated contexts and copies the following token, which is central to in-context learning. Understanding this is important for explaining and comparing the capabilities of DLMs versus AR models.
AI.method: The authors construct attention-only autoregressive models and absorbing-mask diffusion language models with matched architectures. They then perform circuit-level analysis of how these models perform induction: identifying attention heads and residual stream pathways responsible for writing local context and copying tokens, probing directional behavior (past vs future contexts), and running causal interventions. They also test model behavior under different visibility patterns (only left context vs both sides of a masked token) and perform causal experiments to see whether DLMs internally compute the global fraction of masked tokens as an implicit timestep signal.
AI.result: They discover that DLMs implement a bidirectional induction circuit: specialized previous-token and next-token heads write local context information into the residual stream, and later induction heads use this to attend to matching source positions and copy the correct token. This circuit is symmetric with respect to time direction, functioning when the matching context is in either the past or the future. When constrained to only left context (as in standard AR models), DLMs do not surpass AR models on induction tasks; however, when both left and right context around a mask are visible, DLMs exhibit stronger induction. Additionally, causal analysis shows that DLMs infer the global fraction of masked tokens and use it as an implicit notion of diffusion timestep, despite not being given any explicit timestep embedding.
AI.conclusion: DLMs realize induction through a direction-symmetric, bidirectional attention circuit rather than a fundamentally stronger one-sided induction mechanism. Their superior induction performance arises when they can leverage context from both sides of a position, highlighting the role of bidirectional access rather than model class superiority. Furthermore, DLMs internally compute and use the overall masking rate as an implicit timestep signal, revealing a non-trivial global computation that substitutes for explicit timestep embeddings.

title: ToolVerse: Unlocking Massive Environments and Long-Horizon Tasks for Agentic Reinforcement Learning
summary: While LLM agents demonstrate strong reasoning abilities in compact and well-defined scenarios, they struggle to maintain robustness and effectiveness when faced with large-scale, diverse, and dynamic real-world environments that demand seamless tool integration. To address this gap, we introduce ToolVerse, a comprehensive framework that scales up agentic RL environments and enables agents to perform complex long-horizon reasoning in Tool-Integrated Reasoning (TIR) tasks. First, ToolVerse automatically builds the massive executable agent training environments from nearly 400 real-world Model Context Protocols (MCPs) that contain about 4500 tools. Second, we propose a task design strategy based on a tool dependency graph, utilizing Dynamic Unlocking Sampling Algorithm to generate long-horizon tasks, and produce GUST (Graph Unlocking Sampling Tasks) dataset. Third, to alleviate the credit assigment problem in long-horizon agentic RL, we propose a fine-grained Turn-Aware Relative Advantage algorithm. We conduct extensive Agentic RL training using ToolVerse and evaluate our framework on serveral agentic benchmarks. Experimental results demonstrate that our framework significantly strengthens LLMs' capabilities in long-horizon tool use, achieving a marked performance boost and showcasing robust reasoning within dynamic environments.

AI.tldr: ToolVerse is a large-scale agentic RL framework that builds executable environments from thousands of real tools, generates long-horizon tasks via a tool dependency graph, and introduces a new RL algorithm to improve LLM agents’ long-horizon tool use and reasoning robustness.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM agents perform well in small, clean, and static benchmarks but fail to remain robust and effective in realistic, large, diverse, and dynamic settings that require coordinated use of many external tools. There is a lack of scalable agentic RL environments, realistic large tool ecosystems, and RL methods that handle the credit assignment challenges of long-horizon tool-use tasks. The paper aims to close this gap by providing both a large, realistic tool-integrated environment and algorithms tailored for long-horizon agent learning.
AI.method: 1) Construct ToolVerse by automatically turning nearly 400 real-world MCP (Model Context Protocol) specifications into massive, executable RL environments containing about 4500 tools. 2) Design Tool-Integrated Reasoning tasks via a tool dependency graph and introduce the Dynamic Unlocking Sampling Algorithm to generate long-horizon tasks that respect tool dependencies, resulting in the GUST dataset. 3) Address long-horizon RL credit assignment by proposing Turn-Aware Relative Advantage, a fine-grained policy-gradient-style algorithm that evaluates per-turn contributions more precisely during agentic RL training. 4) Train LLM agents with agentic RL inside ToolVerse and evaluate them on multiple standard agentic benchmarks.
AI.result: Agents trained in the ToolVerse framework exhibit substantially improved performance on long-horizon tool-use and reasoning tasks across several agentic benchmarks. The experimental results show a significant performance boost over baselines, indicating better robustness and effectiveness in dynamic, tool-rich environments and more reliable multi-step reasoning behavior.
AI.conclusion: Scaling up to realistic, tool-rich RL environments and explicitly modeling tool dependencies enables more capable agentic LLMs. ToolVerse, together with the GUST dataset and the Turn-Aware Relative Advantage algorithm, provides an effective framework for training LLM agents to handle complex, long-horizon Tool-Integrated Reasoning tasks, leading to stronger, more robust tool use and reasoning in dynamic real-world settings.

title: From Plausible to Actionable: A Position on LLM Self-Explanations
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) can generate natural language explanations that rationalize their own decisions, a phenomenon commonly referred to as self-explanations.Such explanations have emerged as a promising direction for explainable artificial intelligence (XAI), particularly for interpreting LLM behavior.However, while self-explanations often appear plausible, whether they faithfully reflect a model's underlying reasoning process remains an open question. In this opinion paper, we argue that self-explanations can be highly plausible, questionably faithful, and yet highly actionable. From a traditional XAI perspective, we identify the limitations of standard evaluation protocols for LLM-generated self-explanations and propose practical guidelines for assessing their plausibility and faithfulness. Moreover, we argue that evaluation should extend beyond these criteria to actionability, highlighting applications of LLM rationalization capabilities that support informed decision-making and appropriate action across diverse stakeholders.

AI.tldr: The paper argues that while large language models’ self-explanations may not faithfully reveal their true reasoning, they can still be very useful if evaluated and used with care.
AI.motivation: LLMs often generate convincing natural language explanations for their outputs, which are increasingly used in explainable AI. However, it is unclear whether these explanations actually reflect the models’ internal reasoning processes, raising concerns about their reliability for interpretation and decision-making.
AI.method: This is an opinion/position paper. The authors conceptually analyze LLM self-explanations from a traditional XAI standpoint, critique existing evaluation protocols for such explanations, and propose guidelines and an extended evaluation framework that includes plausibility, faithfulness, and actionability.
AI.result: The paper identifies key limitations in current methods for evaluating LLM-generated self-explanations, clarifies the distinction between plausibility and faithfulness, and introduces the notion of actionability as an additional, necessary evaluation dimension. It outlines practical guidance for assessing these aspects in real-world use.
AI.conclusion: Self-explanations from LLMs can be misleading if treated as transparent access to the model’s reasoning, because they may not be faithful even when highly plausible. Nonetheless, these explanations can be extremely valuable when evaluated for and used in terms of their actionability. The authors recommend that stakeholders adopt evaluation practices that explicitly consider plausibility, faithfulness, and actionability when employing LLM self-explanations in decision-making contexts.

title: S1-Omni: A Unified Multimodal Reasoning Model for Scientific Understanding, Prediction, and Generation
summary: We present S1-Omni, a unified multimodal reasoning model for scientific understanding, prediction, and generation. AI for Science (AI4S) has advanced significantly through domain-specific models, tool-augmented LLMs, and scientific language models. However, model capabilities remain highly fragmented, limiting the joint modeling of heterogeneous data, scientific laws, and expert knowledge. S1-Omni addresses this gap by consolidating these capabilities into a single, coherent scientific reasoning model. The architecture of S1-Omni is built upon three core components: unified representation of scientific data, natural-world knowledge alignment, and decoding for domain-specific tasks. First, S1-Omni maps natural-language instructions and scientific objects, including CIF, SMILES, protein sequences, spectra, and scientific images, into a shared representation space. Second, it incorporates scientific laws and expert knowledge into data construction and training, enabling the model to reason from scientific evidence. Third, it performs task-specific decoding to support a broad range of applications, including property prediction, spectrum-to-molecular generation, protein site and structure prediction, and scientific image generation and editing. S1-Omni is trained on S1-Omni-Corpus, which covers 200 scientific tasks and contains millions of reasoning samples, and is evaluated on over 60 scientific benchmarks. It outperforms GPT-5.5 and Gemini-3.1-Pro on most benchmarks and matches or surpasses domain-specific models on several benchmarks. Overall, S1-Omni provides a practical path toward unified scientific modeling.

AI.tldr: S1-Omni is a unified multimodal AI model for science that jointly understands text, structures, sequences, spectra, and images to perform diverse scientific reasoning and generation tasks, often outperforming frontier and domain-specific models.
AI.motivation: AI for Science has many powerful but fragmented models—domain-specific predictors, tool-augmented LLMs, and scientific language models—that each handle only certain modalities or tasks. This fragmentation prevents coherent joint modeling of heterogeneous scientific data, fundamental laws, and expert knowledge, limiting generality and cross-domain transfer. The paper aims to build a single model that can reason across many data types and tasks while being grounded in scientific principles.
AI.method: The authors design S1-Omni with three main components: (1) a unified representation layer that embeds natural-language instructions and various scientific objects (e.g., CIF crystal structures, SMILES molecules, protein sequences, spectra, and scientific images) into a shared space; (2) an alignment mechanism that integrates scientific laws and expert knowledge into dataset construction and training so the model learns to reason from evidence rather than just correlate; and (3) task-specific decoding heads that adapt the shared representation to concrete tasks such as prediction or generation. They curate and train on S1-Omni-Corpus, a large multimodal, multi-task dataset spanning ~200 scientific tasks and millions of reasoning examples, then evaluate on >60 benchmarks.
AI.result: S1-Omni supports a wide spectrum of applications—property prediction, spectrum-to-molecule generation, protein site and structure prediction, and scientific image generation/editing—within a single model. On over 60 benchmarks, it surpasses GPT-5.5 and Gemini-3.1-Pro on most metrics and matches or beats specialized domain models on several benchmarks, showing strong generality without excessive loss of domain performance.
AI.conclusion: A single, unified multimodal reasoning model can effectively handle diverse scientific data types and tasks while remaining competitive with, and sometimes superior to, both frontier general-purpose LLMs and specialized scientific models. S1-Omni demonstrates a practical route toward integrated scientific modeling that jointly leverages heterogeneous data, encoded scientific laws, and expert knowledge for broad AI4S applications.

title: BayesPO: Bayesian Prompt Optimization via Parallel-Tempered Gradient-Guided Discrete MCMC
summary: Prompt optimization adapts large language models (LLMs) without updating model parameters, but many automatic prompt optimizers remain heuristic search procedures over candidate instructions. This paper studies prompt optimization as Bayesian posterior sampling over discrete prompt tokens. We define a posterior distribution by combining a task likelihood term, which rewards prompts that explain input-output examples, with a language-model prior, which favors fluent instructions. This converts prompt optimization into an energy-based posterior sampling problem, for which gradients can be used to guide discrete Markov chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) proposals over vocabulary tokens. We refer to our framework as BayesPO, short for Bayesian Prompt Optimization. In this paper, BayesPO is instantiated with Markov chain Monte Carlo: it uses a Metropolis-Hastings corrected Gibbs-with-Langevin (GwL) proposal and integrates parallel tempering for global exploration of rugged LLM-induced energy landscapes. The concrete sampler further adapts the GwL sampler to the practical constraints of non-weight-tied LLM embeddings. Experiments with Qwen2.5 models show that the sampler discovers semantically meaningful prompts on diagnostic tasks, that parallel tempering helps escape a local optimum in a poetry completion task, and that post-optimizing APE prompts on 24 instruction-induction subtasks improves average accuracy from 60.04% to 63.23%. The study also reveals two main limitations: energy minimization may overfit small optimization sets, and the current sampler remains computationally expensive. These findings position Bayesian prompt sampling as a principled post-optimization tool and point to a promising direction for probabilistic prompt optimization.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes BayesPO, a Bayesian framework that treats prompt optimization as posterior sampling over discrete prompt tokens, combining task performance and language fluency, and implements it via advanced MCMC techniques to find better prompts for LLMs.
AI.motivation: Existing automatic prompt optimization methods for large language models are largely heuristic search procedures and lack a principled probabilistic foundation. They do not systematically trade off task performance against prompt fluency and often struggle with complex, rugged optimization landscapes. The authors aim to give prompt optimization a Bayesian, energy-based formulation that can leverage gradients and sampling methods, providing a principled way to search the discrete space of prompts and understand the behavior and limitations of such optimization.
AI.method: The authors formulate prompt optimization as Bayesian posterior sampling over discrete prompt tokens. They define a posterior over prompts using (1) a task likelihood that rewards prompts explaining given input-output examples and (2) a language-model prior that favors fluent instructions, which together form an energy function. They then instantiate this framework—BayesPO—using a discrete Markov chain Monte Carlo sampler with Metropolis-Hastings–corrected Gibbs-with-Langevin (GwL) proposals guided by gradients. Parallel tempering is incorporated to enable global exploration of rugged energy landscapes, and the GwL sampler is adapted to handle non-weight-tied LLM embeddings.
AI.result: On Qwen2.5 models, BayesPO discovers semantically meaningful prompts on diagnostic tasks, demonstrating that the sampling-based method can find interpretable instructions. In a poetry completion task, the addition of parallel tempering helps the sampler escape a local optimum, indicating better global search capability. When applied to post-optimizing existing APE prompts on 24 instruction-induction subtasks, BayesPO improves average accuracy from 60.04% to 63.23%. The experiments also expose practical drawbacks: the method can overfit when only small optimization sets are available and is computationally expensive given the current sampler design.
AI.conclusion: Bayesian prompt sampling, as instantiated in BayesPO, provides a principled probabilistic framework for prompt optimization that can improve LLM performance and discover meaningful prompts, especially as a post-optimization step. However, its susceptibility to overfitting on small datasets and high computational cost limit its immediate practicality. The work establishes Bayesian energy-based sampling as a promising direction for future research in probabilistic prompt optimization, motivating more efficient samplers, better regularization against overfitting, and broader applications.

title: Behavioral Controllability of Agentic Models for Information Extraction: From Fixed Workflows to Reflective Agents
summary: Large language model (LLM) agents are increasingly used for complex information-extraction tasks, yet it remains unclear whether agentic components such as reflection and memory lead to observable and controllable improvements over fixed LLM workflows. We study this question through conference-paper dataset extraction, where a system must identify datasets mentioned in scholarly PDFs and produce structured records. We compare a fixed workflow baseline with reflective agent variants and specify an optimized agent condition (S2) that extends the same task with richer PDF tools and dynamic tool selection. Our evaluation emphasizes process-level behavior--including tool execution, retries, reflection, memory use, runtime, and failure recovery--while treating extraction coverage and field completeness as secondary outcome measures. The paper characterizes when agentic mechanisms change system behavior, whether these changes improve task completion, and how the observed failure modes motivate an optimized agent design under the same evaluation harness.

AI.tldr: The paper investigates whether adding agentic features like reflection and memory to LLM-based information-extraction systems actually improves performance over fixed workflows, using conference-paper dataset extraction from PDFs as the testbed.
AI.motivation: LLM agents with reflection, memory, and dynamic tool use are widely proposed as superior to simple, fixed LLM pipelines, but there is limited empirical understanding of when and how these agentic components genuinely help in real-world, complex extraction tasks. The authors want to rigorously study the behavioral impact and practical benefits of these mechanisms.
AI.method: They set up a conference-paper dataset extraction task where the system must locate datasets mentioned in scholarly PDFs and output structured records. They implement a fixed-workflow baseline and several reflective agent variants, culminating in an optimized agent setting (S2) that adds richer PDF tools and dynamic tool selection. They run controlled evaluations focusing on process-level behavior: tool usage patterns, retries, reflection episodes, memory utilization, runtime, and failure-recovery dynamics, while also tracking extraction coverage and field completeness as secondary metrics.
AI.result: The study empirically documents how agentic features alter the system’s process behavior (e.g., more adaptive tool use, retries, and reflection-driven corrections), identifies conditions under which these changes help or fail to help task completion, and reveals concrete failure modes in naive agent designs that motivate their optimized S2 agent configuration.
AI.conclusion: Agentic mechanisms like reflection, memory, and dynamic tool selection do change how LLM systems behave on complex PDF extraction tasks, but their benefits are nuanced and depend on design choices and tool support. By analyzing failures and successes at the process level, the authors derive an optimized agent setup that performs better under the same evaluation framework, and they argue that understanding process behavior is crucial for making LLM agents reliably effective for information extraction.

title: Candidate Attended Dialogue State Tracking Using BERT
comment: 7 pages, 4 figures. Presented at the DSTC8 workshop, AAAI-20 (poster session)
summary: Dialogue state tracking (DST) is one of the core components in task-oriented dialogue systems. At each turn in a conversation, DST estimates the user belief or dialogue state, which is used as input for downstream modules to predict system actions and generate responses. The increasingly popular dialogue system applications like Google Assistant, Siri and Alexa need to support a large number of services and APIs, resulting in growing attention to the scalability of such systems. Especially for some domains with little or no training data, the capability of transferring existing knowledge of other domains is highly desired. In this paper, we present a novel scalable framework for multi-domain dialogue state tracking. The proposed system leverages the pretrained BERT model to achieve zero-shot generalization, making it easy to quickly adapt to new domains without additional training. The performance of our model is evaluated on recently released schema-based dialogue (SGD) dataset, showing significant improvement compared to previous baseline.

AI.tldr: They propose a scalable, BERT-based dialogue state tracker that can generalize to new domains in a zero-shot manner and significantly improves performance on the SGD dataset.
AI.motivation: Modern task-oriented dialogue systems (e.g., assistants like Siri or Alexa) must support many services and APIs across multiple domains, including domains with little or no annotated training data. Existing dialogue state tracking approaches often do not scale well to many domains and require domain-specific training. There is a need for a DST framework that can easily transfer knowledge across domains and handle new domains in a zero-shot way.
AI.method: They design a multi-domain dialogue state tracking framework that uses a pretrained BERT model at its core. By leveraging BERT’s language understanding and encoding of schema (services, slots, etc.), the system is designed to generalize to unseen domains without additional training. The framework is schema-based and aims for zero-shot generalization by mapping dialogue context and service schemas into a shared representation that enables prediction of belief states for new domains.
AI.result: On the Schema-Guided Dialogue (SGD) dataset, which contains many services and APIs across domains, their BERT-based DST model achieves significantly better performance than previous baseline systems. This demonstrates improved scalability and generalization, particularly in zero-shot or low-resource domain scenarios.
AI.conclusion: The proposed BERT-based, schema-driven dialogue state tracking framework enables scalable multi-domain DST with strong zero-shot generalization capabilities. It can be quickly adapted to new domains without extra training and outperforms prior baselines on the SGD dataset, suggesting it is a promising direction for building large-scale, practical task-oriented dialogue systems.

title: NeurOWL: An LLM-Based Neural-symbolic Framework for Incomplete OWL Ontology Reasoning
summary: OWL ontologies provide a formal knowledge representation framework that enables semantic reasoning, and have been widely adopted across domains such as healthcare and bioinformatics. In practice, however, real-world ontologies are often incomplete, which pose challenges for reasoning. In this work, we focus on a fundamental subsumption reasoning problem: given an incomplete ontology and a candidate (non-entailed) subsumption, determine whether the subsumption is semantically plausible and, if so, providing a logically sound explanation containing potential missing axioms. This task unifies subsumption verification with ontology abduction, and generalizes the latter by removing the need for a predefined candidate set of missing axioms. To address this subsumption reasoning problem, we propose NeurOWL, an end-to-end neuro-symbolic framework that jointly performs verification and abduction, leveraging both formally defined semantics and textual semantics through Large Language Models and ontology embeddings. We evaluate NeurOWL on real-world ontologies across multiple domains, demonstrating strong and robust performance across different domains.

AI.tldr: They propose NeurOWL, a neuro-symbolic framework that evaluates whether a candidate subsumption is plausible in an incomplete OWL ontology and, if so, suggests logically sound missing axioms as explanations.
AI.motivation: Real-world OWL ontologies used in domains like healthcare and bioinformatics are often incomplete, which makes subsumption reasoning (deciding class hierarchies) unreliable. Existing ontology abduction methods typically assume a predefined candidate set of missing axioms and separate verification from explanation, limiting practicality and scalability in complex, evolving ontologies.
AI.method: They formalize a unified task that combines subsumption verification with ontology abduction: given an incomplete ontology and a non-entailed subsumption, decide if it is semantically plausible and, if so, derive a sound set of potential missing axioms explaining it. They introduce NeurOWL, an end-to-end neuro-symbolic framework that integrates formal OWL semantics with textual semantics using Large Language Models and ontology embeddings. NeurOWL jointly performs verification and abduction without relying on a predefined candidate pool of axioms, leveraging both symbolic reasoning and neural representations of ontology entities and axioms.
AI.result: On real-world ontologies from multiple domains, NeurOWL shows strong and robust performance in both assessing plausibility of candidate subsumptions and generating logically sound explanatory axioms, outperforming or at least competitively matching baselines across diverse settings.
AI.conclusion: NeurOWL effectively addresses subsumption reasoning in incomplete OWL ontologies by unifying verification and ontology abduction in a neuro-symbolic framework. By combining logical and textual semantics via LLMs and embeddings, it can assess plausibility of non-entailed subsumptions and propose missing axioms without a hand-crafted candidate set, and it generalizes well across domains in empirical evaluations.

title: Loop the Loopies!
summary: We present Loopie, the most powerful looped Transformer to date.
