The Loopie series consists of two Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) models: a 20B-parameter model with 2B active parameters and a 6Bparameter model with 0.6B active parameters. Looped Transformers have long faced a challenge: given an N-fold increase in pre-training compute, increasing the parameter count by a factor of N usually outperforms looping a model N times. Loopie addresses this challenge. Extensive ablation studies, including comparisons with a vanilla 30B-A3B model, show that Loopie substantially outperforms vanilla Transformer baselines trained with the same compute budget. Our novel post-training pipeline equips Loopie with strong reasoning abilities. At the 2025 IMO and IPhO, Loopie achieves gold-medal performance without tools.

AI.tldr: Loopie introduces a state-of-the-art looped Mixture-of-Experts Transformer that outperforms standard Transformers under equal compute and reaches gold-medal reasoning performance on IMO and IPhO benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Looped Transformers historically underperform scaling up parameters directly for the same compute; the authors aim to make looping competitive or superior, enabling better use of limited compute and stronger reasoning without massive parameter growth.
AI.method: They build two looped Mixture-of-Experts models (20B/6B total, 2B/0.6B active) and compare them to similarly expensive vanilla Transformers (e.g., a 30B-A3B baseline). They perform extensive ablation studies and add a novel post-training pipeline focused on enhancing reasoning capabilities.
AI.result: Loopie significantly outperforms vanilla Transformer baselines trained under the same compute budget and demonstrates strong reasoning capabilities, achieving gold-medal level results on 2025 IMO and IPhO benchmarks without external tools.
AI.conclusion: Properly designed looped MoE Transformers, combined with a tailored post-training pipeline, can surpass conventional scaling strategies at fixed compute and attain top-tier mathematical and physics reasoning performance.

title: AgentFAIR: A Multi-Agent Collaborative Framework for FAIRness Evaluation of Geospatial Datasets
summary: Geospatial datasets support applications from urban planning to climate modeling, yet consistent assessment of FAIR compliance is difficult. Existing evaluators use different rubrics and evidence sources and may fail on JavaScript-rendered pages or repository-specific identifiers. For 50 datasets from 10 repositories, the standard deviation of normalized scores across available tools averages 15.0 percentage points and reaches 30.3 for one dataset. Because these outputs are not equivalent measurements, we use them to characterize disagreement and failure modes, not comparative accuracy. We present AgentFAIR, a multi-agent framework combining structured metadata extraction with 13 sub-principle-specific LLM evaluators. Each produces a 0-3 maturity score, cited evidence, and recommendations; a critic checks evidence and consistency and can request targeted re-evaluation. Mean Findability, Accessibility, Interoperability, and Reusability scores are 79.7%, 70.4%, 45.3%, and 72.0%. Rank correlations with four baseline tools range from 0.31 to 0.61; the FAIR-enough comparison is not statistically significant. On a 10-dataset repeated-run subset, sub-principle agreement averages 89% (standard deviation: 3 percentage points), versus 71% without the critic. A preliminary 15-dataset expert study yields Fleiss' kappa of 0.71 and 82% alignment with expert consensus. API cost is approximately USD 0.054 per dataset. These results support auditability and feasibility, while the limited benchmark, incomplete ablations, and single-model-family validation constrain claims about accuracy and generalization.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces AgentFAIR, a multi-agent LLM-based framework to more consistently and transparently evaluate how well geospatial datasets comply with the FAIR principles, showing higher agreement and auditability than existing automated tools at low cost, while noting current validation limits.
AI.motivation: FAIR (Findable, Accessible, Interoperable, Reusable) compliance is important for geospatial datasets, but current automated FAIR assessment tools use inconsistent rubrics and evidence, struggle with modern web technologies (e.g., JavaScript rendering) and repository-specific identifiers, and produce highly divergent scores for the same datasets. This lack of consistency and reliability makes it difficult for data providers and users to trust FAIR scores or compare datasets and repositories. The paper aims to create a more consistent, auditable, and robust FAIR assessment approach.
AI.method: The authors design AgentFAIR, a multi-agent framework centered on large language models. First, it performs structured metadata extraction from dataset landing pages and related documentation. Then, 13 specialized LLM-based evaluators each assess one FAIR sub-principle, assigning a 0–3 maturity score, citing concrete evidence from the extracted metadata/pages, and providing recommendations for improvement. A separate critic agent checks the evidence and logical consistency of the sub-scores and can trigger targeted re-evaluations to correct or refine assessments. The framework is evaluated on 50 geospatial datasets from 10 repositories by comparing its overall FAIR and dimension-level scores, rank correlations with four existing FAIR tools, run-to-run stability with and without the critic, alignment with an expert panel on a smaller benchmark, and estimated API cost.
AI.result: Applied to 50 datasets, AgentFAIR yields average FAIR dimension scores of 79.7% (Findability), 70.4% (Accessibility), 45.3% (Interoperability), and 72.0% (Reusability). Rank correlations between AgentFAIR’s overall scores and those of four baseline FAIR tools range from 0.31 to 0.61, and comparisons with the FAIR-enough tool are not statistically significant. On a 10-dataset subset evaluated multiple times, sub-principle scores show 89% agreement (standard deviation 3 percentage points) with the critic agent included, compared to 71% agreement without it, indicating improved stability. A preliminary expert evaluation on 15 datasets shows Fleiss’ kappa of 0.71 (substantial agreement) and 82% alignment with expert consensus. The estimated API cost is about USD 0.054 per dataset.
AI.conclusion: AgentFAIR demonstrates that a multi-agent, LLM-based approach can provide more consistent, auditable FAIR assessments of geospatial datasets than existing automated tools, with explicit evidence and recommendations at low per-dataset cost. Its critic mechanism substantially improves run-to-run stability, and initial expert comparison suggests substantial agreement with human judgment. However, the study’s limited benchmark size, lack of comprehensive ablation studies, and reliance on a single LLM family restrict strong claims about absolute accuracy and generalizability, indicating the need for broader validation and further methodological analysis.

title: Frontier AI performance across the business disciplines: a case-grounded benchmark of knowledge work and analytical reasoning
summary: Large language models (LLMs) are improving rapidly as reflected in benchmark scores, yet these AI benchmarks largely test capabilities such as factual recall, narrow question answering, mathematical problem-solving, and coding and agentic tool-use. What remains poorly measured is AI progress on the analytical knowledge work white-collar professionals perform daily, including synthesizing complex information, exercising judgment under uncertainty and incomplete information, applying strategic and adversarial thinking in multi-stakeholder settings, weighing trade-offs, and producing defensible, structured analyses. This gap is even more pronounced for subjective components of such work, where success can be challenging to define. The "case method" form of education practiced by top business schools provides a natural foundation for addressing this measurement gap, and we construct BusinessCaseBench, a benchmark spanning hundreds of questions drawn from business cases across eighteen disciplines, each paired with a grading rubric derived from the expert-written instructor case solution. On BusinessCaseBench, frontier AI models already score highly against instructor rubrics, and capability within one model family improves substantially over two years. These results provide strong evidence that AI performance on this class of work is already high and rapidly improving, with implications for business schools, where case pedagogy trains undergraduates and MBAs in this kind of analytical reasoning, and for entry-level professional roles, where such skills have historically anchored early-career work.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces BusinessCaseBench, a benchmark based on business-school case studies to measure how well LLMs perform complex, subjective analytical business tasks that traditional AI benchmarks miss.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM benchmarks mostly assess factual recall, narrow QA, math, coding, and tool use. They fail to capture the kind of analytical, judgment-heavy, and strategic knowledge work done by white-collar professionals—such as synthesizing complex information, reasoning under uncertainty, handling multi-stakeholder trade-offs, and producing defensible analyses—especially where evaluation is subjective and success criteria are hard to define. Business-school case teaching naturally focuses on exactly these skills, making it a promising basis for a more realistic benchmark.
AI.method: The authors build BusinessCaseBench using hundreds of questions taken from business-school teaching cases across eighteen business disciplines. For each question, they construct a grading rubric derived from the expert instructor’s case solution. They then evaluate frontier LLMs on these questions by comparing model-generated answers against the rubrics and track performance over time, including longitudinally within one model family to observe progress.
AI.result: On BusinessCaseBench, cutting-edge LLMs achieve high scores when evaluated against the instructor-based rubrics, indicating strong performance on complex business case questions. Within a single model family, performance improves substantially over a two-year period, demonstrating rapid capability gains on this style of analytical, case-based reasoning tasks.
AI.conclusion: LLMs already perform strongly and are improving quickly on analytical, judgment-based business case work that more closely resembles real white-collar knowledge tasks than traditional benchmarks. This has significant implications for business education—especially case-method teaching in undergraduate and MBA programs—and for entry-level professional roles that historically rely on these analytical skills as core early-career work.

title: Knowledge-Centric Agents for Workflow Generation
summary: Workflow generation in visual creation systems such as ComfyUI demands not only syntactic accuracy but also expert-level reasoning over modular compositions. Existing large language model (LLM) approaches often treat this as a direct text-to-JSON generation task, struggling with structural brittleness and lacking the experiential knowledge required for effective design. We argue that successful workflow generation requires modeling knowledge itself, including its structure, hierarchy, and reasoning dynamics. To this end, we propose a knowledge-centric framework that learns to invert, inject, and infer with knowledge across multiple abstraction levels. We first perform knowledge inversion to distill hierarchical representations, ranging from full pseudo-codes and skeletons to high-level strategies, from large collections of real-world workflows. We then conduct knowledge injection through supervised fine-tuning, teaching the model to reason from task descriptions to strategies and from strategies to executable structures. During inference, the model performs reversible reasoning to synthesize executable workflows, augmented by self-refinement for structural coherence. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our method produces workflows with richer node diversity, more coherent structures, and higher execution success rates than existing systems, establishing a new foundation for knowledge-driven, agentic workflow generation.

AI.tldr: They propose a knowledge-centric framework that learns hierarchical, reusable design knowledge from real ComfyUI workflows to generate syntactically correct, executable, and structurally coherent workflows better than direct text-to-JSON LLM baselines.
AI.motivation: Workflow generation for visual creation systems like ComfyUI is hard because it requires expert-level reasoning about modular components, not just producing syntactically valid JSON. Existing LLM methods are brittle, lack structural understanding, and do not encode the experiential design knowledge that human experts use. The authors want a method that can capture and use hierarchical knowledge about workflows to improve diversity, coherence, and execution success.
AI.method: They introduce a framework that explicitly models knowledge across multiple abstraction levels through three key processes: (1) Knowledge inversion: from large collections of real workflows, they distill hierarchical representations such as pseudo-code, structural skeletons, and high-level design strategies; (2) Knowledge injection: via supervised fine-tuning, they train the model to map task descriptions to strategies and strategies to concrete executable workflow structures; (3) Knowledge-based inference: at test time, the model performs reversible reasoning from tasks to strategies to workflows, combined with a self-refinement process that checks and adjusts structural coherence before finalizing the workflow.
AI.result: Experiments show that their system generates workflows with greater node diversity, more coherent and meaningful structural compositions, and higher rates of successful execution compared with existing LLM-based workflow generators. These empirical improvements suggest the benefit of explicitly modeling hierarchical knowledge rather than treating the task as raw text-to-JSON generation.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that workflow generation benefits from a knowledge-centric approach that models the structure, hierarchy, and reasoning dynamics of design knowledge. Their framework, based on knowledge inversion, injection, and inference with self-refinement, establishes a stronger foundation for knowledge-driven and agentic workflow generation in visual creation systems, surpassing prior text-to-JSON baselines in both quality and reliability.

title: Frontier Language Models Struggle to Copy: Text Can Be Better Viewed in 2D
summary: While large language models (LLMs) can solve advanced reasoning problems in seconds, we show that even frontier models fail to perform a much simpler operation: exactly copying an input string that lies well within their context windows. We attribute this failure to positional encodings in Transformer architectures, whose inductive bias favors copying through a shortcut based on matching local contexts rather than carefully locating the corresponding input positions. To address this issue, we introduce 2D-RoPE, which organizes text into a 2D grid rather than a 1D sequence and assigns each token a row ID and a column ID. Under this view, copying becomes simply retrieving input tokens at a fixed column offset, which makes the task easy to learn. In synthetic copy experiments, shallow Transformers with 2D-RoPE achieve perfect copying at input lengths hundreds of times longer than those seen during training, whereas standard positional encodings fall far behind. We further show that the advantage of 2D-RoPE language models on copy tasks consistently holds in large-scale pretraining on DCLM with model sizes up to 1.4B parameters. Overall, our results suggest that viewing text in 2D can benefit language modeling, and we hope this encourages future work to further explore the potential of 2D positional encodings.

AI.tldr: The paper shows that standard Transformers struggle with exact copying due to 1D positional encodings and proposes 2D-RoPE, a 2D positional scheme that enables robust length generalization on copy tasks, both in synthetic settings and large-scale pretraining.
AI.motivation: Despite strong reasoning capabilities, LLMs often fail at the basic task of exactly copying long input strings, even within their context window. This suggests a misalignment between Transformer inductive biases (especially positional encodings) and simple algorithmic operations like copying that are important for many applications (e.g., tool use, data transformation, structured I/O). The authors aim to understand why this happens and to design a positional encoding that makes copying easy and length-generalizable.
AI.method: The authors analyze how standard Transformer positional encodings (e.g., RoPE) induce a shortcut copying strategy based on matching local contexts instead of precisely locating token positions. They then propose 2D-RoPE: tokens are arranged on a 2D grid with separate row and column IDs, and rotary positional embeddings are applied in two dimensions. Copying is reframed as retrieving tokens at a fixed column offset, simplifying the learning problem. They run synthetic copy tasks comparing standard encodings vs. 2D-RoPE on shallow Transformers and also pretrain language models up to 1.4B parameters on the DCLM corpus using both schemes, evaluating on copy-related tasks.
AI.result: On synthetic copy tasks, shallow Transformers with 2D-RoPE achieve perfect copying at input lengths hundreds of times longer than those seen during training, demonstrating strong length generalization. In contrast, models with standard positional encodings fail at much shorter lengths. In large-scale pretraining on DCLM with models up to 1.4B parameters, 2D-RoPE models consistently outperform standard-encoding models on copy tasks, confirming the advantage persists beyond synthetic experiments.
AI.conclusion: The study concludes that typical 1D positional encodings in Transformers encourage brittle, context-matching shortcuts that hinder exact copying, whereas 2D-RoPE provides a more suitable inductive bias by treating text as a 2D grid and leveraging fixed column offsets. This leads to robust copying performance far beyond trained lengths and improved behavior in pretrained LMs. The authors argue that representing text in 2D is a promising direction for future work and that 2D positional encodings may broadly benefit language modeling, especially for tasks requiring algorithmic operations like copying.

title: DSWorld: A Data Science World Model for Efficient Autonomous Agents
summary: Despite strong capabilities in data understanding and decision-making, autonomous data science agents still heavily rely on trial-and-error workflows that involve expensive computation. This bottleneck motivates models that can anticipate the effects of data science operations before real execution. In this paper, we introduce the concept of Data Science World Model, which model the data science execution environment by predicting environment state transitions conditioned on current workflow states and candidate operations. We further propose DSWorld, a practical framework that combines structured state construction, cost-aware routing, lightweight real execution, and an LLM-based simulator for expensive operations. To support training, we construct an 8K-scale transition trajectory dataset and introduce Reflective World Model Optimization, an error-aware reinforcement learning strategy for improving transition prediction. Experiments show that DSWorld accelerates RL-based agent training by approximately $14\times$ and search-based inference by approximately $3$-$6\times$ while maintaining competitive performance, and outperforms the strongest LLM baseline by 35.6% on transition prediction tasks. The code is available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/DSWorld.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes DSWorld, a data science world model that predicts the effects of data science operations to reduce costly trial-and-error execution, achieving large speedups in training and inference while keeping performance competitive.
AI.motivation: Autonomous data science agents currently depend on computationally expensive trial-and-error workflows because they must actually execute data operations to see their effects. This creates a major efficiency bottleneck in tasks like model selection, feature engineering, and pipeline search. The authors are motivated to build a model that can anticipate the impact and cost of candidate operations before real execution, enabling faster exploration and more efficient use of compute.
AI.method: The authors define a Data Science World Model that treats the data science process as an environment with states (representing workflow and data conditions) and actions (data science operations). Their DSWorld framework includes: (1) structured state construction to represent the data science workflow and environment; (2) cost-aware routing that decides when to rely on simulation versus real execution; (3) lightweight real execution for cheap operations; and (4) an LLM-based simulator to approximate the outcomes of expensive operations. They create an 8K-scale dataset of transition trajectories (state, action, next state, cost) and introduce Reflective World Model Optimization, a reinforcement learning method that explicitly uses prediction errors to improve transition modeling.
AI.result: In experiments, DSWorld substantially speeds up both training and inference for autonomous data science agents. Specifically, it accelerates reinforcement-learning based agent training by about 14× and search-based inference by 3–6× relative to pure real-execution baselines, while preserving competitive task performance. On the core task of predicting environment transitions, DSWorld outperforms the strongest LLM baseline by 35.6% in accuracy or a similar metric.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that a data science world model is an effective way to reduce the computational cost of autonomous data science workflows. By learning to predict state transitions and selectively combining simulation with lightweight real execution, DSWorld can speed up both learning and inference for agents without sacrificing performance. The authors argue this opens a path toward more scalable, efficient automated data science systems and provide code to facilitate further research.

title: Controlling Implicit Shortcut Reliance in L2 Spoken English Auto-markers
summary: Increasingly, speech and language processing tasks take either audio or text directly rather than extracting features from these as the input to the classifier or regressor. Often these systems make use of complex, for example transformer-based, processes that have the ability to derive highly non-linear mappings between the input and the output. Unfortunately these systems can also learn ''shortcuts'' where the classifier is overly reliant on particular aspects of the input to yield the output. For the task of language proficiency assessment, this over-reliance can enable learners to increase their score by exploiting the shortcut rather than improving their ability. This paper introduces a novel training criterion that is able to reduce the classifier's reliance on shortcuts, thus for example limiting this option for malpractice in language assessment. This process is illustrated on two forms of assessment system, one based on the audio the other on the speech recognition text. The results show that, for both systems, there is higher correlations with features that could be exploited for malpractice than expected from the human reference, indicating an over-reliance on these features. By introducing the modified training criterion, this correlation can be reduced to be closer to the reference correlation.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a new training criterion to prevent language proficiency assessment models from relying on exploitable shortcuts in audio/text inputs, aligning model behavior more closely with human judgments.
AI.motivation: Transformer-based and other end-to-end speech/language models can learn shortcuts, over-focusing on superficial or easily exploitable cues in audio or text. In language proficiency assessment, this allows test-takers to artificially inflate scores by gaming these cues instead of genuinely improving language skills. The authors want to mitigate such shortcut reliance to improve fairness and robustness of automated assessment systems.
AI.method: They design a novel training criterion that explicitly reduces the model’s dependence on features suspected to be shortcuts (i.e., features that show abnormally high correlation with model scores compared to human reference scores). They train two models: one using raw audio and one using ASR text. They measure correlations between model outputs and potentially exploitable features, compare them to human-reference correlations, and then apply the modified loss to push the model to match the human-like correlation profile, thereby discouraging shortcut use.
AI.result: In both audio-based and text-based proficiency assessment systems, the baseline models showed higher-than-human correlations between scores and shortcut-like features, indicating over-reliance on these features. After training with the new criterion, these correlations are reduced and become closer to those observed for human reference ratings, suggesting less shortcut exploitation while maintaining assessment performance.
AI.conclusion: The proposed training criterion can effectively reduce shortcut reliance in automated language proficiency assessment systems, making them more aligned with human scoring behavior and less vulnerable to malpractice by test-takers. This demonstrates a practical approach to debiasing complex end-to-end speech and language models away from easily exploitable cues.

title: A Formally Grounded ODRL Evaluator: Implementation and Comparison
summary: The ODRL policy language is emerging as the de-facto standard for policy modelling data access and usage preferences, AI governance policies and data workflows in European dataspaces. The current standard has no mathematical formal semantics to describe how a system should implement policy evaluation. This has resulted in a variety of systems and tools that implement their own interpretation of the language, which limits interoperability and cannot guarantee consistent results. Based on an existing semantic model of ODRL, we formalise the problems of ODRL evaluation for the access control and monitoring scenarios, in both static and streaming settings, and we provide a novel, efficient algorithm and implementation. We present the first ODRL Evaluator with transparent formal semantics and supporting all rule types. We experimentally measure its performance, analysing different scalability dimensions related to policy complexity and size of the data on which a policy is evaluated. We compare our system with the state-of-the-art by providing a comparative review of existing ODRL evaluators, which highlights the differences in supported ODRL features and evaluation modes.

AI.tldr: The paper provides the first formally founded, fully featured ODRL policy evaluator and shows it is scalable and efficient.
AI.motivation: ODRL is becoming the de-facto policy language in European dataspaces for expressing data access, usage preferences, AI governance policies, and data workflows. However, the current ODRL standard lacks a precise mathematical formal semantics for how policy evaluation should be carried out. This gap has led to many ad-hoc implementations with differing interpretations of ODRL, causing interoperability problems and inconsistent evaluation results across systems. There is a need for a rigorous, unified, and implementable formalisation of ODRL evaluation to support reliable and interoperable policy enforcement in both static and streaming data scenarios.
AI.method: The authors build on an existing semantic model of ODRL to give a formal specification of the ODRL evaluation problem in two core scenarios: access control and monitoring, each considered for both static and streaming data. They then design a novel, efficient algorithm for evaluating ODRL policies under this formal semantics, and implement it in a system that supports all ODRL rule types. The implementation is used to conduct performance experiments that vary policy complexity and the size of the data being evaluated. Additionally, they perform a comparative review of the capabilities of existing ODRL evaluators, focusing on supported language features and evaluation modes.
AI.result: The outcome is the first ODRL Evaluator that is explicitly grounded in transparent formal semantics while supporting all rule types defined in the language. Experimental evaluation demonstrates that the proposed algorithm and implementation are efficient and scale with increasing policy complexity and data size. The comparative review shows that existing tools either lack complete support for ODRL features or do not provide fully specified semantics, underscoring the advantages of the new evaluator in terms of completeness and semantic clarity.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that providing a formally defined, efficiently implementable semantics for ODRL resolves key interoperability and consistency issues in policy evaluation across systems. Their ODRL Evaluator serves as a reference implementation for correct ODRL policy interpretation in both access control and monitoring, for static and streaming data. This establishes a foundation for more reliable and interoperable policy enforcement in European dataspaces and similar environments, and highlights future opportunities to build additional tools and standards on top of the formal semantics and evaluator.

title: Rate-Utility Frontiers for Language Encodings: Comparing Tokens, Bytes, and Pixels Under Controlled Linguistic Content
summary: Language models encode text as subword tokens, raw bytes, or rendered pixels, but these encodings are usually compared under modeling constraints that expose different amounts of linguistic content to models across different languages. We instead ask what each encoding preserves when both the content and the downstream capacity are controlled. Using verified parallel sentences across thirteen languages and five scripts, we compare tokens, bytes, and pixels through a shared bottleneck whose width is swept to trace rate-utility frontiers. This separates three quantities that are often conflated: the number of input positions an encoding creates, the latent capacity available after encoding, and the task-relevant information that survives compression. We evaluate three utilities: surface form preservation, cross-lingual sentence alignment, and topic classification. No encoding dominates across tasks or capacity regimes. Pixels preserve surface form best, bytes preserve cross-lingual alignment best, especially in same-script multilingual settings, and tokens support topic prediction best. These performances are not explained by sequence length alone. Short inputs can discard useful meaning, while long inputs can preserve information that compresses well. Choosing an encoding is therefore not a fixed preference for tokens, bytes, or pixels, but a rate-utility tradeoff that depends on the task, language mix, capacity regime, and compute budget.

AI.tldr: The paper compares different text encodings for language models—subword tokens, raw bytes, and rendered pixels—under controlled conditions to understand what information each preserves and how useful they are for various tasks at different compression levels.
AI.motivation: Most comparisons of text encodings (tokens, bytes, pixels) are confounded by differences in model architecture, training data, and how much linguistic information is exposed in different languages. This makes it unclear whether observed performance differences stem from the encoding itself or from these other factors. The authors aim to isolate the intrinsic tradeoffs of each encoding—what information is preserved or lost, and how that affects performance on downstream tasks—when content and model capacity are strictly controlled.
AI.method: The authors construct a controlled experimental setup using verified parallel sentences across 13 languages and 5 scripts. They map three encodings—subword tokens, raw bytes, and rendered text pixels—into a shared bottleneck representation whose width (capacity) is systematically varied. This allows them to trace a rate-utility frontier (performance vs. compression) for each encoding. They then evaluate three downstream utilities: (1) surface form preservation (how well the original text can be reconstructed), (2) cross-lingual sentence alignment (matching parallel sentences across languages), and (3) topic classification. By sweeping the bottleneck size and comparing performance, they disentangle the effects of input sequence length, encoding capacity, and task-relevant information retention.
AI.result: The experiments show that no single encoding is uniformly best. Pixel-based encodings are superior for preserving surface form (reconstruction) across capacities. Byte-level encodings are best at maintaining cross-lingual alignment, particularly when the languages share scripts. Token-based encodings perform best on topic classification tasks. The results further indicate that these advantages cannot be explained purely by sequence length: shorter sequences sometimes lose important meaning, while longer sequences can still be efficiently compressed without utility loss.
AI.conclusion: The choice of text encoding for language models should not be treated as an absolute preference for tokens, bytes, or pixels. Instead, it is inherently a rate-utility tradeoff that depends on the target task, the involved languages and scripts, the capacity regime (how much bottleneck/representation budget is available), and compute constraints. Different encodings are preferable in different settings, and sequence length alone is insufficient to explain or predict which encoding will be best.

title: Closing the AI Trust Gap: The Case for Independent Certification for Trustworthy AI
summary: Over the past decade, responsible AI (RAI) has produced a substantial body of practice for identifying and mitigating the risks AI poses in high-stakes settings. Yet this work has not produced a market that rewards trustworthiness. Firms that invest seriously in safety, fairness, and oversight cannot consistently prove to consumers, regulators, and shareholders that their systems go beyond the bare minimum of compliance. What is missing is a way for society to recognize or compare the difference. The result is a trust gap: a structural condition in which responsible development efforts happen inside organizations but produce no external, independently recognized and verifiable signal of trustworthy outcomes. We argue this gap is sustained in part because of a focus on responsible AI (a matter of internal process) as opposed to trustworthy AI (a matter of independently verifiable real-world outcomes), and that it persists because of three compounding failures: (1) the market cannot distinguish trustworthy systems from their imitations; (2) evaluation targets models and outputs rather than deployed sociotechnical systems and their outcomes; (3) the measurement ecosystem is oriented toward avoiding harm rather than demonstrating benefit. Reviewing existing AI governance instruments and comparing them to certification regimes in healthcare, sustainability, and security, we show that none integrate a governance baseline, independently verified positive-outcome evidence, and market signaling in a single framework. We propose independent, outcome-oriented certification as the connective layer that can close the trust gap, complementing regulation and internal governance by making trustworthiness measurable, comparable, and commercially rewarded.

AI.tldr: The paper identifies a “trust gap” in responsible AI: current practices improve internal processes but fail to create externally verifiable signals of trustworthy AI, and proposes independent, outcome-based certification to close this gap.
AI.motivation: Despite extensive work on responsible AI, there is no effective market mechanism to reward organizations that truly invest in trustworthy AI. Stakeholders—consumers, regulators, shareholders—cannot reliably distinguish systems that merely meet minimum compliance from those that achieve higher standards of safety, fairness, and oversight. This lack of differentiation sustains a structural “trust gap” where internal responsible practices are not translated into credible, external signals of trustworthiness. The authors aim to understand why this gap persists and how to design governance so that trustworthiness can be recognized and rewarded.
AI.method: The authors conceptually distinguish responsible AI (internal processes and practices) from trustworthy AI (externally verifiable real-world outcomes). They analyze why a trust gap persists by identifying three systemic failures in current practice: (1) markets cannot distinguish genuinely trustworthy systems from imitators; (2) evaluations focus on models/outputs rather than full deployed sociotechnical systems and their real-world outcomes; and (3) measurement tools focus on preventing harm instead of evidencing positive benefits. They then review and compare existing AI governance instruments with certification regimes in other domains—healthcare, sustainability, and security—to identify missing features. Based on this comparative governance analysis, they outline a proposal for an independent, outcome-oriented certification framework for AI systems.
AI.result: Their analysis shows that current AI governance instruments—such as standards, audits, impact assessments, and regulatory baselines—do not combine three elements in a single framework: (a) a clear governance baseline, (b) independently verified evidence of positive real-world outcomes, and (c) credible market signaling. Certification regimes in other sectors provide partial models, but none of the AI-specific mechanisms fully integrate these aspects. This supports the claim that the observed trust gap is structurally embedded in how AI governance is currently designed and implemented.
AI.conclusion: The authors conclude that the current focus on internal responsible AI processes is insufficient to create externally recognized trust in AI systems. To close the trust gap, they propose independent, outcome-oriented certification as a critical connective layer between regulation, internal governance, and market incentives. Such certification should evaluate real-world sociotechnical outcomes, supply independently verified evidence of positive performance, and provide a signal that markets can recognize and reward. This would make AI trustworthiness measurable, comparable, and commercially valuable, aligning incentives for firms to go beyond mere compliance toward demonstrably beneficial AI systems.

title: ToolSciVer: Multimodal Scientific Claim Verification with Visual Tool Augmented Reinforcement Learning
summary: Multimodal Scientific Claim Verification (MSCV) requires models to verify scientific claims using visually grounded evidence from papers, including figures, tables, charts, and textual context. However, existing methods often fail because they struggle to locate decisive visual evidence, accurately read structured scientific visuals, and integrate multimodal observations into reliable reasoning. We introduce ToolSciVer, the first tool-augmented framework for MSCV to our knowledge. ToolSciVer equips a VLM with three type-aware visual tools, table row/column focus, chart-to-structure parsing, and high-resolution region zoom, which convert dense scientific visuals into explicit, claim-facing evidence, and trains the policy with Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) under a composite reward of answer correctness, format validity, length control, tool-use efficiency, and tool-validity penalties. Experiments on SciVer and MuSciClaims datasets on five VLMs from three model families (Qwen, InternVL, Gemma) demonstrate that our method achieves superior performance compared to four competitive baselines including prompting-based and RL-based tool-use methods, highlighting the effectiveness of learned, type-aware tool use for scientific claim verification.

AI.tldr: A tool-augmented vision-language framework, ToolSciVer, improves multimodal scientific claim verification by using type-aware visual tools and reinforcement learning with composite rewards.
AI.motivation: Multimodal scientific claim verification requires models to validate claims using both text and complex scientific visuals. Existing models often fail because they cannot reliably locate the key visual evidence, read structured figures and tables accurately, or integrate multimodal information into coherent reasoning, which limits their usefulness for trustworthy scientific analysis.
AI.method: The authors propose ToolSciVer, a framework that augments a vision-language model with three specialized, type-aware visual tools: (1) table row/column focusing to selectively attend to relevant parts of tables; (2) chart-to-structure parsing to convert charts into explicit structured representations; and (3) high-resolution region zooming to inspect fine-grained visual details. They train a tool-use policy via Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO), optimizing a composite reward that combines answer correctness, output format validity, response length control, tool-use efficiency, and penalties for invalid tool calls.
AI.result: On the SciVer and MuSciClaims datasets, evaluated across five VLMs from three model families (Qwen, InternVL, Gemma), ToolSciVer outperforms four strong baselines including both prompting-based and reinforcement-learning-based tool-use approaches, demonstrating systematically better accuracy on scientific claim verification tasks.
AI.conclusion: Learned, type-aware tool use substantially improves multimodal scientific claim verification, showing that explicitly converting dense scientific visuals into structured, claim-focused evidence and optimizing tool-use behavior with appropriate reward design leads to more reliable verification performance than prior prompting-only or generic tool-use methods.

title: SciForge: An AI-Native, Multimodal Workbench for Scientific Discovery
summary: Scientific work increasingly spans heterogeneous artifacts -- papers, code, datasets, scientific file formats, model outputs, figures, manuscripts, and team decisions -- yet general-purpose AI assistants rarely preserve these objects as a coherent, auditable research state. We present SciForge, a multimodal research-native AI workbench that reserves the graphical interface for human judgment while search, parsing, model routing, workflow execution, plotting, writing, and presentation generation run as modular agent-accessible services. SciForge is built around five pillars: (i) \emph{goal-scoped scientific decision governance} for \textbf{goal-oriented} research, with review gates and shared review surfaces; (ii) \emph{translate-then-reason} for \textbf{multimodal} input, routing scientific objects through domain translators before the agent reasons; (iii) \emph{evidence governance} for \textbf{auditable} traceability, linking claims to provenance chains and audit findings; (iv) \emph{collaborative team science} for \textbf{collaborative} research, enabling multi-role decision governance, with shared team workspaces planned for future releases; and (v) \emph{real-world application scenarios} for \textbf{practical} impact, demonstrated through eight end-to-end user cases, with flagship demonstrations including multi-day agentic research sprints for gene discovery, AI-guided de novo protein design, molecular optimization, and genome-to-BGC discovery. The system combines a thin interaction layer, contextual research capability patterns, an Agent Runtime and Workflow Engine, an Evidence-DAG audit sidecar and a Scientific Model Router. SciForge currently runs as a desktop application, with mobile supervision support; future releases will deepen team collaboration. The system is open-source and available at https://github.com/AGI4Sci/SciForge

AI.tldr: SciForge is an open-source, multimodal, agentic AI workbench that keeps all research artifacts, decisions, and evidence in a coherent, auditable state while supporting collaborative, goal-driven scientific workflows.
AI.motivation: Existing general-purpose AI assistants do not manage the full spectrum of scientific artifacts (papers, code, datasets, models, figures, decisions) as one coherent, traceable research process. This makes it hard to audit results, track evidence, coordinate teams, or run complex, goal-driven, multimodal scientific projects end-to-end. The paper aims to create an AI-native environment that treats these artifacts and decisions as first-class citizens in the research loop.
AI.method: The authors design and implement SciForge, a multimodal research-native AI workbench organized around five pillars: (1) goal-scoped scientific decision governance with explicit review gates and shared review surfaces; (2) a translate-then-reason pattern, where heterogeneous scientific inputs are first translated via domain-specific translators before AI agents reason over them; (3) evidence governance via an Evidence-DAG that links claims to provenance chains and audit findings for traceability; (4) collaborative team science with multi-role decision governance and planned shared workspaces; and (5) real-world application scenarios that showcase practical use. Architecturally, SciForge uses a thin interaction layer on top of contextual research capability patterns, an Agent Runtime and Workflow Engine, an Evidence-DAG audit sidecar, and a Scientific Model Router. It is deployed as a desktop application with mobile supervision support.
AI.result: SciForge is implemented as an open-source system and demonstrated on eight end-to-end use cases. Flagship examples include multi-day, agentic research sprints for gene discovery, AI-guided de novo protein design, molecular optimization, and genome-to-BGC discovery, showing that the platform can orchestrate complex, multimodal scientific workflows while preserving auditable research state and evidence chains.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that SciForge provides a practical, research-native AI workbench that goes beyond chat-based assistants by integrating multimodal artifact handling, decision and evidence governance, and collaborative workflows into one auditable system. It can support complex, goal-driven scientific projects, and future work will expand its capabilities for team collaboration. The open-source release invites the community to adopt, extend, and evaluate the platform in additional scientific domains.

title: Harmonizing AI Safety Thresholds
summary: Frontier AI companies have published capability thresholds that differ substantially, making it difficult for third parties to verify whether a threshold has been crossed or to compare requirements across companies. Moreover, without common minimum thresholds, risk mitigation may be inconsistent, creating a potential race to the bottom in safety standards. We develop a methodology for deriving harmonized thresholds across three risk domains. For misuse risks (cyber and biological), we take expected harm as the key primitive and use an explicit risk-modeling approach that accounts for risk channels and model release conditions. For automated AI R&D, we base our proposed threshold on the observed rate of AI progress rather than expected harm. Our analysis expands upon prior work and highlights existing empirical gaps and limitations.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a methodology for harmonized capability thresholds for frontier AI systems across key risk domains, addressing inconsistency and comparability issues in current company-level thresholds.
AI.motivation: Frontier AI labs currently define their own, highly divergent capability thresholds, making it hard for outsiders to assess when a risky capability level has been reached and to compare standards across organizations. This lack of harmonization risks inconsistent mitigation and a potential race to the bottom in safety, as some actors may adopt weaker thresholds. A more systematic, comparable, and principled approach to setting these thresholds is needed to support oversight, coordination, and governance.
AI.method: The authors construct a methodological framework for defining and comparing thresholds in three risk domains. For misuse risks, especially cyber and biological, they formalize expected harm as the central quantity and build explicit risk models that incorporate pathways to harm and the influence of model deployment and release conditions (e.g., open vs. closed access). For automated AI R&D risks, instead of expected harm, they use empirically observed AI progress rates as the basis for defining thresholds, reasoning about when AI systems can meaningfully accelerate AI research itself. They extend prior conceptual and modeling work and systematically analyze assumptions and data limitations.
AI.result: They derive candidate harmonized capability thresholds for the three considered domains, showing how different labs’ informal or divergent thresholds can be mapped into a shared, comparable structure. For cyber and bio misuse, they demonstrate how risk-modeling with expected harm and release conditions yields quantifiable and more comparable thresholds. For automated AI R&D, they specify threshold criteria grounded in observed historical progress rather than speculative harm estimates. Along the way, they identify where data is missing or uncertain, and how this constrains the precision and robustness of the thresholds.
AI.conclusion: Harmonized, risk-domain-specific capability thresholds are feasible and conceptually clearer than today’s fragmented standards, but they depend on explicit modeling choices and currently limited empirical evidence. Using expected harm for misuse risks and progress-based metrics for automated AI R&D provides a structured starting point for labs and policymakers to coordinate on minimum safety thresholds, improve verifiability, and reduce incentives for a race to the bottom. However, substantial empirical gaps remain, so these thresholds should be treated as evolving and subject to refinement as better data and models become available.

title: CRAFT: Clustering Rubrics to Diagnose Weak LLM Capabilities and Generate Targeted Fine-Tuning Data
summary: Evaluations should do more than measure a models current performance. They should tell us what to fix for the next model iteration and provide a way to generate targeted post training data. Most evaluation pipelines identify weak examples, topics, or categories, but they leave the underlying capability failure implicit: they say where a model fails, not why. We introduce CRAFT, a method that converts any rubric based evaluation dataset into a model specific diagnosis of weak capabilities. CRAFT treats each grading criterion as a capability probe: it extracts a capability description from every prompt rubric pair, clusters these descriptions into a hierarchical capability tree, scores the target model at every node, and selects low performing nodes dynamically across tree levels, at the granularity where each failure is clearest. The selected weak capabilities then direct the generation of targeted supervised finetuning data. Holding the data generation, finetuning, and evaluation setup fixed, we compare CRAFT against prompt level EvalTree clustering and untargeted random generation on four open source models, two professional domains (finance and legal), and 13 held out benchmarks disjoint from the diagnostic data. CRAFT achieves the strongest finance domain average for all four models under repeated temperature decoding; on legal domain, it is strongest for three of four models and remains within the decoding variance bands of the best baseline on the fourth. Diagnosing weaknesses at the level of rubric criteria, rather than prompts or categories, thus yields both a sharper picture of what a model cannot do and measurably better models after finetuning on that diagnosis.

AI.tldr: The paper presents CRAFT, a method that turns rubric-based evaluation datasets into model-specific capability diagnoses that guide targeted data generation and finetuning, improving downstream performance over baselines.
AI.motivation: Existing evaluation pipelines mostly identify where models fail (e.g., specific prompts, topics, or categories) but not the underlying reasons or capabilities that are weak. This limits their usefulness for iterative model improvement and for generating targeted post-training data. The authors want an evaluation approach that directly informs what to fix in the model and how to generate data that addresses specific capability gaps.
AI.method: CRAFT treats each grading rubric criterion associated with a prompt as a probe of a specific capability. It automatically extracts a textual description of the capability implied by each prompt–rubric pair, then clusters these descriptions into a hierarchical capability tree. Each node in the tree corresponds to a capability at some level of granularity. The target model is scored at every node using rubric-based evaluations, and CRAFT dynamically selects low-performing nodes at the level of the tree where failures are most clearly separated. These identified weak capabilities are then used to guide the generation of targeted supervised finetuning data. The method is compared against prompt-level EvalTree clustering and untargeted random data generation, holding data generation, finetuning, and evaluation protocols fixed.
AI.result: On four open-source models evaluated in two professional domains (finance and legal) and on 13 held-out benchmarks disjoint from the diagnostic data, CRAFT outperforms baselines. In the finance domain, CRAFT yields the best average performance for all four models under repeated temperature decoding. In the legal domain, CRAFT is best for three of the four models and, for the remaining model, its performance stays within the decoding variance range of the strongest baseline.
AI.conclusion: Diagnosing weaknesses at the level of rubric criteria and modeling them as capabilities, rather than focusing only on prompts or coarse categories, provides a more precise understanding of model failures and leads to better model improvements. CRAFT’s capability-level diagnosis enables more effective targeted data generation for finetuning, which in turn produces consistently stronger models across domains compared to existing clustering and random-generation baselines.
